title: Twitter's Recommendation Algorithm
abstract: The canonical blog post describing Twitter's full recommendation pipeline, published alongside the March 2023 open-source release. Details the three-stage architecture: Candidate Sourcing, Ranking, and Heuristics/Filtering. The system processes ~500M daily tweets, narrowing to ~1,500 candidates per request, running ~5 billion times per day in under 1.5 seconds. Candidate sourcing splits into in-network sources (RealGraph edge weights + Earlybird search index, producing ~50% of For You tweets) and out-of-network sources (SimClusters community detection with 145,000 communities, TwHIN heterogeneous information network embeddings using contrastive loss on positive/negative triplets, and GraphJet real-time bipartite user-tweet graph powering ~15% of feed). The heavy ranker is a ~48M-parameter parallel MaskNet variant outputting 10 engagement probability labels. The final score is a weighted combination with hardcoded weights (e.g., P(favorite) x 0.5, P(good_click_and_reply) x 11). The Home Mixer serving layer is built on the Product Mixer framework. Also describes the light ranker for pre-filtering and visibility/content filtering layers.
unique_contribution: The only official end-to-end description of the complete Twitter recommendation pipeline at the time of open-sourcing, with specific model sizes, candidate counts, and scoring formulas.

title: A New Era of Transparency for Twitter
abstract: Companion blog post to the algorithm open-source release explaining Twitter's rationale for publishing its recommendation code. Details what was included in the release (the core recommendation algorithm code including candidate generation, ranking, and filtering) and critically what was excluded: training data, model weights, ad recommendation code, and some trust and safety code. The post frames the decision as a transparency initiative, acknowledging that code alone without training data and weights does not fully enable external reproduction. Discusses the broader philosophy of algorithmic transparency and Twitter's commitment to allowing public scrutiny of its systems. Provides context for why certain components were withheld, primarily citing user privacy concerns for training data and competitive/safety considerations for other excluded components.
unique_contribution: Official documentation of the scope and limitations of the open-source release, clarifying what is and is not available for external analysis.

title: Using Deep Learning at Scale in Twitter's Timelines
abstract: Foundational 2017 blog post from Twitter's Cortex team describing the transition from decision trees and logistic regression to deep neural networks for timeline ranking. The system scores tweets for individual users based on predicted engagement. Features are divided into three entity groups: tweet characteristics (recency, media presence, interaction counts), author information (past interactions, relationship strength), and user behavior (historical engagement patterns). Key technical innovations include discretization of sparse feature values before feeding into the deep network, a custom sparse linear layer with online normalization to prevent gradient explosion, per-feature bias to distinguish absent vs. zero-valued features, an isotonic calibration layer for probability recalibration, and a sampling scheme for positive/negative training example balance. The move to deep learning achieved significant gains in offline accuracy and online A/B test metrics including tweet engagement and time spent. The post emphasizes production requirements: quality, speed, resource efficiency, and maintainability.
unique_contribution: First detailed account of Twitter's transition to deep learning for timeline ranking, with specific architectural innovations for handling sparse features at scale.

title: Embeddings@Twitter
abstract: A 2018 blog post from Twitter's Cortex team describing the company's strategy for making embeddings a first-class citizen within the ML platform. Covers the development of embedding models, related tooling, and infrastructure to support diverse use cases across Twitter's products. Discusses various embedding types used at Twitter including user embeddings, content embeddings, and entity embeddings, trained using methods inspired by word2vec and related techniques adapted for social graph and engagement data. The post positions embeddings as a foundational layer that multiple downstream models and services can leverage, reducing redundant feature engineering across teams. Describes the infrastructure challenges of generating, storing, serving, and updating embeddings at Twitter's scale. This work laid groundwork for later embedding systems like SimClusters and TwHIN that became central to the recommendation algorithm.
unique_contribution: Establishes Twitter's philosophy of embeddings as shared infrastructure and previews the embedding-centric approach that later defined the recommendation system.

title: Twitter Meets TensorFlow
abstract: Describes Twitter's migration of its ML infrastructure from Lua Torch to TensorFlow, centered on the DeepBird framework developed by the Cortex team. DeepBird v2 expects data in Twitter's DataRecord format with training scripts in Python using TensorFlow and custom extensions. The migration was motivated by TensorFlow's superior production serving support, HDFS compatibility, extensive documentation, and large community compared to Lua Torch. Previously, deep network architecture required manual YAML specification of 152 layers; TensorFlow enabled parameterized Python loops for efficient architecture definition. The post discusses warm starting (continuing training from previous weights rather than random initialization), which achieved equivalent quality with only 20% of training data and 0.4% improvement in relative log loss. A split network architecture divides features into tweet, author, and user groups with separate sub-networks before concatenation, reducing training time by ~22% while improving quality. TensorFlow's profiler resolved initial 10x slowdowns. The system serves hundreds of millions of users, scoring thousands of tweets per user.
unique_contribution: Details the DeepBird framework and split network architecture that became the basis for Twitter's ranking models, plus practical lessons on warm starting and framework migration.

title: Productionizing ML with Workflows at Twitter
abstract: Describes ML Workflows, a tool developed by Cortex to automate, schedule, and share machine learning pipelines at Twitter. The system was designed to reduce maintenance costs of production ML pipelines, improve engineering productivity, and increase experimentation rate. After evaluating open-source alternatives including Luigi and Azkaban, as well as internal solutions, the team selected Aurora Workflows and Apache Airflow as the frontrunners. The Timelines Quality team adopted ML Workflows and reduced the model retraining-to-deployment interval from four weeks to one week, with A/B tests confirming that shorter retraining intervals improved timeline quality and ranking. The post covers the full ML lifecycle from dataset preparation to experimentation to production deployment, and discusses challenges of dependency management, scheduling, monitoring, and failure recovery in production ML pipelines.
unique_contribution: Documents Twitter's ML workflow automation, with concrete evidence that reducing retraining intervals from 4 weeks to 1 week improved ranking quality.

title: Graph ML at Twitter
abstract: Covers Twitter's application of graph-based machine learning techniques for recommendation and content understanding. Discusses SimClusters, a community detection system published at KDD 2020 that identifies ~145,000 communities using a Metropolis-Hastings algorithm computed offline on MapReduce/Hadoop. SimClusters uses Sparse Binary Factorization to map users and content into the same embedding space, enabling tweet candidate generation via approximate nearest neighbor lookup within community clusters. Also covers TwHIN (Twitter's Heterogeneous Information Network), which learns dense knowledge graph embeddings for users, tweets, and advertisements using the TransE approach with contrastive loss on (source entity, edge type, target entity) triplets. RealGraph is described as a directed, weighted graph capturing engagement signals (days since edge creation, days since last interaction, number of common friends, explicit/implicit engagement statistics). GraphJet maintains a dynamic, real-time, undirected bipartite graph between users and tweets in memory on a single server.
unique_contribution: Primary documentation of SimClusters and TwHIN, the two major embedding systems that power out-of-network candidate generation in the recommendation pipeline.

title: A New Approach: Metric Learning for SplitNet
abstract: Describes the application of metric learning techniques to Twitter's SplitNet model used in the light ranking stage of the online advertising system. SplitNet represents users and ads as fixed-length embeddings under strict latency constraints. The metric learning approach jointly learns user embeddings, ad embeddings, and a similarity matrix from data, modifying the last layer of SplitNet to add parameters for learning the similarity matrix. This replaced a simpler dot-product similarity measure. Online A/B experiments showed the metric learning technique improved App Install ad conversion rate by 11%. The post provides context on the two-stage ad ranking pipeline: a light ranker (SplitNet) for fast pre-filtering of candidates using embedding-based scoring, followed by a heavy ranker for final ad selection. Discusses the tradeoff between model expressiveness and serving latency in the light ranking stage.
unique_contribution: Demonstrates how metric learning improved ad ranking by 11% conversion rate, and documents the SplitNet architecture used in Twitter's ad light ranker.

title: Streaming Logging Pipeline of Home Timeline Prediction System
abstract: Documents the redesign of Twitter's training data pipeline for the home timeline prediction system from batch to streaming architecture. The new system adopted a Kappa streaming architecture using Apache Kafka and KafkaStreams as the processing engine, replacing offline batch processing. The core processing logic joins served tweet features with engagement labels (likes, retweets, replies) to generate training examples. The team built a custom join library because KafkaStreams' default join function did not meet their exact requirements. The migration reduced model refresh time from 7 days to approximately 1 day, significantly improving model quality by training on more recent data. The streaming-first approach collects and prepares all training data in real-time without delay, enabling continuous model updates. The post discusses challenges of streaming joins including late-arriving events, feature-label alignment, and maintaining exactly-once semantics.
unique_contribution: Shows how moving from batch to streaming training data reduced model staleness from 7 days to 1 day, with concrete architecture details of the Kappa-based pipeline.

title: What Twitter Learned from the RecSys 2020 Challenge
abstract: Summarizes Twitter's learnings from sponsoring and analyzing the ACM RecSys 2020 Challenge, where they released a dataset of 160 million public tweets for training and 40 million for validation/testing over a two-week period. The challenge asked participants to predict probabilities of four engagement types: Like, Reply, Retweet, and Quote Tweet. Key insights from winning teams include: (1) GPU acceleration enabled the Nvidia team to run entire pipelines including feature engineering in 2 minutes 18 seconds vs. hours on CPU; (2) extensive feature crossing was critical for performance, with detailed feature importance analysis across engagement types; (3) handling distribution shift between training and test sets was addressed by building discriminator models to predict train-test differences. The post discusses how these learnings inform Twitter's internal approaches to engagement prediction and the value of external competition for validating internal methods.
unique_contribution: Provides insights from external teams tackling Twitter's engagement prediction problem, including feature importance rankings and GPU-accelerated training approaches.

title: Model-Based Candidate Generation for Account Recommendations
abstract: Describes Twitter's move from heuristic-based to model-based candidate generation for the account recommendation (Who To Follow) product. The system uses a two-stage pipeline: candidate generation (retrieving hundreds to thousands of relevant accounts) followed by a ranking phase using a ML model. Previously, dozens of candidate sources had been developed over a decade, each based on narrow slices of user information using collaborative filtering or graph expansion, resulting in less personalized recommendations. The new model-based framework employs a two-tower neural network architecture: one tower for consumption behaviors (what content a user engages with) and another for production behaviors (what content a user creates). Embedding features from both towers enhance recommendation relevance. The post discusses how this framework generalizes across different recommendation contexts and enables more personalized account suggestions beyond simple social graph proximity.
unique_contribution: Documents the two-tower architecture for account candidate generation, showing the shift from heuristic to learned retrieval for Who To Follow.

title: A Hybrid Approach to Personalize Notification Volume
abstract: Describes Twitter's ML-driven approach to personalizing push notification frequency (push-caps) for recommended content. Recognizes that users have different preferences for notification volume and uses ML to find the optimal amount per user. The approach combines two models: (1) a hidden Markov model (HMM) that creates customer cohorts based on login and notification open behavior, with grid search to find optimal push-caps per cohort, and (2) a multilayer perceptron (MLP) that models individual login and reachability behavior, feeding into a utility maximization framework to compute personalized push-caps. In production, a hybrid approach combines push-caps from both models, using whichever performs better for each cohort. The HMM cohort approach provides a lightweight, interpretable baseline, while the MLP approach offers finer personalization for cohorts where the grid search is suboptimal.
unique_contribution: Details the HMM + MLP hybrid approach for notification volume personalization, a rarely documented aspect of recommendation systems.

title: Accelerating Ad Product Development at Twitter
abstract: Documents the decomposition of Twitter's monolithic AdServer into product-vertical microservices by the Revenue Platform team. Twitter Ads grew from ~15 engineers and $28M revenue in 2010 to hundreds of engineers and ~$3.4B revenue in 2019, with the monolithic architecture becoming a development bottleneck. The solution involved product decomposition to isolate each product vertical (e.g., Video ads), allowing engineers to make changes without affecting orthogonal products. Twitter developed a Data Dependency Framework making dependencies explicit and removing mutation of shared data structures, with each processor becoming fully independent given predefined inputs. This explicit contract helped form meaningful logical APIs and boundaries for new libraries. The post discusses the organizational and architectural challenges of scaling ad systems, including the transition from a small platform team serving as a force multiplier to a larger organization needing decoupled services.
unique_contribution: Documents the AdServer decomposition and Data Dependency Framework, providing context for how Twitter's ad serving architecture evolved.

title: Introducing our Responsible Machine Learning Initiative
abstract: Announces Twitter's company-wide Responsible Machine Learning (RML) initiative led by the ML Ethics, Transparency and Accountability (META) team. The interdisciplinary working group includes engineers, researchers, data scientists, and members from trust and safety and product teams. Outlines three initial focus areas: (1) gender and racial bias analysis of the image cropping (saliency) algorithm, (2) fairness assessment of Home timeline recommendations across racial subgroups, and (3) analysis of content recommendations for different political ideologies across seven countries. The post emphasizes that technical solutions alone cannot resolve harmful effects of algorithmic decisions, requiring cross-functional collaboration. Commits to sharing learnings through peer-reviewed research, data insights, and descriptions of both successful and unsuccessful approaches. Represents the institutional framework that produced the subsequent image cropping bias study and algorithmic amplification research.
unique_contribution: Establishes the institutional framework (META team, RML initiative) under which Twitter's algorithmic fairness research was conducted.

title: Examining Algorithmic Amplification of Political Content on Twitter
abstract: Presents findings from a massive-scale experiment studying algorithmic amplification of political content on Twitter, published as a companion to a PNAS paper. The study used a long-running randomized experiment with nearly 2 million daily active accounts, comparing algorithmically-ranked timelines against a reverse-chronological control group. Examined political parties in seven countries (Canada, France, Germany, Japan, Spain, UK, US) and analyzed 6.2 million news articles shared in the US. Key findings: (1) in six of seven countries, the mainstream political right received higher algorithmic amplification than the mainstream political left; (2) in the US media landscape, algorithmic amplification favored right-leaning news sources; (3) contrary to prevailing belief, the study did not find evidence that algorithms amplify far-left and far-right groups more than moderate ones. The study represents the most comprehensive public audit of an algorithmic recommender system's effects on political content at the time.
unique_contribution: Largest-scale public study of political content amplification in a recommender system, with surprising finding that far-left/far-right content is not preferentially amplified.

title: Sharing Learnings About Our Image Cropping Algorithm
abstract: Reports findings from Twitter's internal analysis of bias in its saliency-based image cropping algorithm, introduced in 2018. The algorithm used a neural network trained on human eye-tracking data to predict saliency scores across image regions, selecting the highest-scoring point as the crop center. Testing on randomly linked images of diverse people revealed systematic biases: ~8% skew toward displaying women over men, and ~4% skew toward lighter-skinned faces over darker-skinned faces. Additionally, the algorithm occasionally cropped to body parts (chest, legs) rather than faces, particularly for women. The saliency model's biases appeared embedded from training data (open-source eye-tracking datasets reflecting human gaze patterns). Twitter's response: began testing full aspect-ratio display without algorithmic cropping on iOS and Android, received positive feedback, and launched the change to all users. The key conclusion was that image cropping is a decision best left to users rather than an algorithm. The analysis code was open-sourced for external reproduction.
unique_contribution: Rare primary-source documentation of discovering and responding to ML bias in production, with quantified bias metrics and the decision to remove the algorithm entirely.

title: Introducing Twitter's First Algorithmic Bias Bounty Challenge
abstract: Announces the industry's first algorithmic bias bounty competition, held as part of DEF CON AI Village from July 30 to August 6, 2021. Following Twitter's May 2021 publication of its image cropping saliency algorithm bias analysis and the release of the analysis code, this challenge invited the ethical AI community to identify additional biases and potential harms in the saliency algorithm. Cash prizes ranged from $500 to $3,500. The initiative extended the traditional security bug bounty concept to algorithmic fairness, recognizing that external researchers may identify harms that internal teams miss. The post describes the challenge structure, rules, and evaluation criteria, positioning it as a pilot for potential future bias bounty programs across other Twitter ML systems.
unique_contribution: Documents the first-ever algorithmic bias bounty program, establishing a new paradigm for external algorithmic auditing.

title: Sharing Learnings from the First Algorithmic Bias Bounty Challenge
abstract: Reports results from Twitter's first algorithmic bias bounty challenge. The winning submission used a counterfactual approach to demonstrate that the saliency model encodes stereotypical beauty standards, preferring slimmer, younger, feminine, and lighter-skinned faces. The second-place submission confirmed age bias by showing the algorithm rarely selects people with white hair as most salient in multi-face images, and also studied spatial gaze bias in group photos with people with disabilities. The third-place entry analyzed linguistic bias favoring English over Arabic script in memes. The most innovative prize went to a demonstration that the model prefers emojis with lighter skin tones. Root cause analysis suggests biases are embedded in the core saliency model through training data (open-source human eye-tracking data reflecting conscious and unconscious human biases). Twitter committed to implementing the evaluation rubric internally as part of model risk assessments, enhancing focus on representational harms.
unique_contribution: Concrete findings from external bias auditing, identifying age, beauty standard, linguistic, and skin-tone biases in the saliency model through diverse analytical approaches.

title: Investing in Privacy Enhancing Tech to Advance Transparency in ML
abstract: Describes Twitter's investment in privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs) to enable external algorithmic auditing while protecting user privacy. Led by the ML Ethics, Transparency, and Accountability (META) team, the initiative aims to develop privacy-preserving methods for external researchers to access non-public Twitter data and to internally democratize the Responsible ML Workbench. Twitter partnered with OpenMined, an open-source nonprofit experienced in deploying PETs in healthcare and the public sector, to test privacy-preserving machine learning methods. The goal is enabling researchers outside Twitter to conduct analyses on the same data used internally, going beyond the public API. The post discusses the tension between algorithmic transparency (requiring data access) and user privacy, positioning PETs as the technical bridge. Methods discussed include differential privacy, federated learning, and secure multi-party computation as potential approaches for privacy-preserving data access.
unique_contribution: Documents the novel approach of using PETs to enable external algorithmic auditing, a unique solution to the transparency-privacy tradeoff.

title: The Infrastructure Behind Twitter: Scale
abstract: Comprehensive overview of Twitter's infrastructure evolution and scale as of 2017. Twitter transitioned from third-party colocated hosting to private infrastructure beginning in 2010, deploying custom data centers across five continents. Network architecture evolved from deep buffer ToR switches with carrier-grade core switches (2010) to a Clos network topology using BGP for routing (2015), providing smaller failure domains, horizontal bandwidth scaling, and reduced CPU overhead. Storage and messaging comprise 45% of the infrastructure footprint, including Manhattan (Cassandra-based distributed database), FlockDB (MySQL sharding), Twemcache and Redis for caching, Haplo for timeline caching, DistributedLog for stream feeding to Heron, and Hadoop/Vertica for analytics. Scale metrics include hundreds of clusters delivering 320M packets/second aggregate rate and 120GB/s to clients. Orchestration uses Apache Mesos with nearly all caching migrated from bare metal. Configuration management via Puppet with 100+ monthly committers, 500+ modules, and 1,000+ roles.
unique_contribution: Most comprehensive overview of Twitter's physical and systems infrastructure with specific scale metrics, providing essential context for understanding system constraints.

title: TSAR, a TimeSeries AggregatoR
abstract: Introduces TSAR (TimeSeries AggregatoR), a flexible, reusable end-to-end service architecture built on top of Summingbird for time-series aggregation problems such as counting tweet impressions. Summingbird provides the hybrid computational framework combining Hadoop batch processing with Storm stream processing; TSAR adds a higher-level abstraction for analytics pipelines. Three design principles: (1) separation of event production from aggregation, where the first stage extracts events from source data and the second stage buckets and aggregates, with TSAR standardizing the aggregation stage; (2) unified data schema specified in a datastore-independent way, with TSAR mapping schemas onto diverse datastores and handling schema evolution; (3) integrated service toolkit connecting data processing, warehousing, query, observability, and alerting components with automatic configuration and orchestration. TSAR infers key-value data models and relational database schema descriptions from a job-specific configuration file, automating Twitter best practices for time-series aggregation.
unique_contribution: Documents the TSAR framework for time-series aggregation, a foundational component used for feature computation in the recommendation pipeline.

title: Processing Billions of Events in Real Time at Twitter
abstract: Describes Twitter's event processing infrastructure handling approximately 400 billion events daily generating petabyte-scale data. Event sources span diverse platforms and storage systems including Hadoop, Vertica, Manhattan distributed databases, Kafka, Twitter EventBus, GCS, BigQuery, and PubSub. The Data Platform team built internal tools: Scalding for batch processing, Heron for streaming, TSAR for hybrid batch/real-time processing, and Data Access Layer for discovery and consumption. The post details challenges with Heron at scale: backpressure during high event volumes causes bolts to slow due to garbage collection costs, accumulating spout lag indicating high latency. The solution involved migrating from a Lambda architecture to a hybrid architecture deployed across both Twitter's data centers and Google Cloud Platform. This migration achieved low latency, high accuracy, stability, architectural simplicity, and reduced operational costs for real-time event processing.
unique_contribution: Documents the scale of Twitter's event processing (400B events/day) and the migration from Lambda to hybrid architecture across on-prem and GCP.

title: Powering Real-Time Data Analytics with Druid at Twitter
abstract: This blog post describes Twitter's adoption and deployment of Apache Druid for real-time data analytics at scale. Druid is a high-performance, column-oriented, distributed data store designed for sub-second OLAP queries on event-driven data. Twitter uses Druid to power real-time dashboards, monitoring, and analytics across various product and infrastructure teams. The post likely covers the architecture of their Druid deployment, how it integrates with Twitter's existing data infrastructure (including ingestion from Kafka streams), query performance characteristics, and the scale of data processed. It would also discuss operational challenges of running Druid at Twitter's scale, including cluster management, data retention policies, and how Druid compares to or complements other analytics systems in Twitter's stack such as TSAR and other time-series tools.
unique_contribution: Details on how Twitter deployed Apache Druid for real-time analytics at massive scale, filling the gap between streaming systems and batch analytics.

title: Manhattan, Our Real-Time Multi-Tenant Distributed Database for Twitter Scale
abstract: This blog post introduces Manhattan, Twitter's in-house distributed key-value database designed to replace multiple legacy storage systems. Manhattan was built to serve as a general-purpose, real-time, multi-tenant storage system capable of handling Twitter's demanding latency and throughput requirements. The post covers Manhattan's architecture, including its layered storage engine design, support for multiple consistency models (eventual and strong consistency), multi-tenancy with isolated namespaces, and its ability to serve both low-latency reads and high-throughput writes. Manhattan stores critical data including user timelines, social graph data, and metadata that feed into the recommendation pipeline. The system was designed to handle tens of millions of queries per second with single-digit millisecond latencies, replacing earlier systems like FlockDB and T-bird. It uses a pluggable storage backend architecture.
unique_contribution: Introduces Manhattan, the foundational storage layer that serves user data, social graph, and features consumed by Twitter's recommendation pipeline.

title: Strong Consistency in Manhattan
abstract: This blog post describes how Twitter added strong consistency guarantees to Manhattan, their distributed key-value database. While the initial Manhattan release supported eventual consistency through last-writer-wins conflict resolution, many use cases required stronger guarantees. The post covers the implementation of a Paxos-based consensus protocol within Manhattan to provide linearizable reads and writes for specific namespaces that require them. It discusses the trade-offs between consistency and availability in a distributed system, how Twitter handles leader election, quorum management, and failure recovery. The post also addresses the performance implications of strong consistency, including increased write latency due to consensus rounds, and how they mitigated these through careful co-location of replicas and batching optimizations. This capability is important for features like counters, rate limiters, and certain user state that the recommendation system depends on.
unique_contribution: Details the Paxos-based strong consistency layer added to Manhattan, enabling reliable counters and state that feeds into recommendation features.

title: Data Transfer in Manhattan Using RocksDB
abstract: This blog post describes how Twitter improved data transfer and replication efficiency in Manhattan by adopting RocksDB as a storage engine backend. The post covers the technical challenges of transferring large volumes of data between Manhattan nodes during rebalancing, node replacement, and cluster expansion. By leveraging RocksDB's SST file format and its ability to perform bulk ingestion, Twitter significantly reduced the time and resource cost of data migrations. The post discusses the integration architecture, how RocksDB's compaction and compression features were tuned for Manhattan's workload characteristics, and the performance improvements achieved. It covers topics like SST file shipping, incremental backups, and how this approach reduced the operational burden of maintaining Manhattan clusters at Twitter's scale, which handles tens of billions of key-value pairs across thousands of nodes.
unique_contribution: Describes the RocksDB integration into Manhattan for efficient bulk data transfer, a key operational improvement for the storage layer underlying the recommendation system.

title: How We Scaled Reads on the Twitter Users Database
abstract: This blog post from 2023 describes how Twitter scaled its users database to handle the read traffic demands of the platform. The users database is one of the most critical and heavily queried datastores at Twitter, as user profile information is needed for virtually every API call and recommendation request. The post likely covers the caching strategies, read replicas, and query optimization techniques used to handle millions of reads per second. It may discuss the migration from older storage systems, the role of Manhattan and/or MySQL in serving user data, how read traffic is distributed across replicas, and the monitoring and alerting infrastructure that ensures low-latency responses. This is particularly relevant to the recommendation system since user features (follower counts, account age, verification status, engagement history) are key inputs to ranking models.
unique_contribution: Provides insight into how user feature data is served at scale, directly relevant to understanding the latency constraints of the recommendation pipeline's feature fetching.

title: Introducing Gizzard: A Framework for Creating Distributed Datastores
abstract: This blog post introduces Gizzard, Twitter's open-source framework for building distributed datastores. Gizzard sits as a middleware sharding layer between application code and storage backends, providing automatic partitioning, replication, and fault tolerance. The framework uses a tree-based sharding topology where data is partitioned into shards that can be replicated and migrated transparently. Gizzard handles job scheduling for write operations with an at-least-once delivery guarantee, making writes eventually consistent. It supports multiple storage backends (MySQL, Lucene, Redis, etc.) and was used to build several Twitter systems including FlockDB (social graph), T-bird, and others. The post covers the sharding strategy, replication topology, fault handling through job queues, and the middleware architecture pattern. Though largely superseded by Manhattan by 2014, Gizzard represents an important historical piece of Twitter's storage infrastructure evolution.
unique_contribution: Describes Twitter's early distributed storage framework that underpinned systems like FlockDB (social graph), a predecessor to the graph-based recommendation infrastructure.

title: Announcing Snowflake
abstract: This blog post announces Snowflake, Twitter's distributed unique ID generation service. As Twitter moved from a single MySQL database to a distributed architecture, generating unique, roughly time-ordered IDs became critical. Snowflake generates 64-bit unique IDs composed of a timestamp (milliseconds since a custom epoch), a machine/worker ID, and a sequence number. This structure ensures IDs are roughly sortable by creation time without requiring coordination between ID-generating nodes, enabling high throughput (tens of thousands of IDs per second per node) with very low latency. The time-ordered property is essential for Twitter's timeline features, as tweets can be sorted by ID without additional timestamp lookups. Snowflake IDs are used throughout Twitter's infrastructure including in the recommendation pipeline where tweet IDs encode temporal information used for recency-based ranking and candidate filtering.
unique_contribution: Defines the Snowflake ID scheme that encodes temporal ordering into tweet IDs, directly used by the recommendation system for time-based filtering and sorting.

title: Flying Faster with Twitter Heron
abstract: This blog post introduces Heron, Twitter's real-time stream processing engine built as a successor to Apache Storm. Heron was designed to address Storm's limitations in debugging, performance predictability, and resource efficiency. The post covers Heron's architecture which uses a process-based model (instead of Storm's thread-based model) for better isolation and debugging, a backpressure mechanism that prevents cascading failures, and compatibility with Storm's API for easy migration. Heron achieved significant improvements: 3x throughput improvement, 5-10x reduction in end-to-end latency, and substantial reduction in hardware resources. At Twitter, Heron processes real-time signals used by the recommendation system including user engagement events, real-time features, and streaming aggregations that feed into models like RealGraph. The system handles billions of events per day across Twitter's infrastructure.
unique_contribution: Describes Heron, the stream processing engine that computes real-time features and engagement signals consumed by Twitter's recommendation models.

title: Caching with Twemcache
abstract: This blog post introduces Twemcache, Twitter's fork of Memcached optimized for their specific caching workloads. Twemcache adds several features over stock Memcached including per-slab eviction policies (random eviction, LRU per slab, and a novel 'slab automove' mechanism), better memory management to reduce slab calcification, and improved observability through detailed per-slab statistics. The post discusses Twitter's caching challenges at scale, where cache misses directly impact tail latency for user-facing features including timeline rendering and recommendation serving. Twemcache is used to cache user profiles, social graph data, tweet metadata, and computed features that the recommendation system relies on for low-latency inference. The post covers the slab allocation strategies, eviction policies, and how Twemcache handles the hot-key problem common at Twitter's scale where popular tweets or users create extreme cache access patterns.
unique_contribution: Describes the caching layer that enables low-latency feature lookups critical to serving recommendations within Twitter's <1.5 second latency budget.

title: Cassovary: A Big Graph-Processing Library
abstract: This blog post introduces Cassovary, Twitter's open-source JVM-based graph processing library designed for processing graphs with billions of edges on a single machine. Unlike distributed graph processing frameworks like Pregel or GraphX, Cassovary is optimized for in-memory graph computation on a single server, using compact memory representations to fit large graphs in RAM. The library supports directed and undirected graphs, provides iterators for graph traversal (BFS, DFS, random walks), and implements algorithms like PageRank and label propagation. At Twitter, Cassovary was used for social graph analysis, computing features like TweepCred (PageRank-based user reputation) and supporting Who-To-Follow recommendations. The library's ability to process Twitter's follower graph (billions of edges) in-memory makes it a key component for computing graph-based signals used in the recommendation pipeline. Written in Scala, it leverages efficient array-based adjacency representations.
unique_contribution: Describes the graph processing library used for computing TweepCred and other graph-based features that feed into Twitter's recommendation ranking models.

title: Building DistributedLog: Twitter's High-Performance Replicated Log Service
abstract: This blog post describes DistributedLog, Twitter's high-performance replicated log service built on top of Apache BookKeeper. DistributedLog provides a durable, replicated, ordered log abstraction that supports both low-latency writes (single-digit millisecond durability) and high-throughput tailing reads. The system uses BookKeeper's ledger-based storage for durability and replication, adding a log stream abstraction with log segment management, reader positioning, and namespace management. DistributedLog serves as a building block for several Twitter systems including Manhattan (for write-ahead logging), real-time event pipelines, and pub-sub systems. The post covers the architecture, consistency guarantees, performance optimizations (write pipelining, batching), and how it achieves fault tolerance through quorum-based writes. It handles billions of records per day at Twitter and provides the durability guarantees needed for the event streams that feed real-time recommendation features.
unique_contribution: Describes the replicated log service underpinning Manhattan's durability and the real-time event streams that feed the recommendation pipeline.

title: Search Relevance Infrastructure at Twitter
abstract: This blog post describes the search relevance infrastructure at Twitter, covering how search results are ranked and returned to users. The post discusses the architecture of Twitter's search stack, including the Earlybird real-time search index, the blender service that merges results from multiple Earlybird clusters, and the relevance models that rank search results. It covers feature extraction for search ranking (text features, social features, engagement signals, recency), the machine learning models used for relevance scoring, and the serving infrastructure that delivers results within strict latency constraints. The search relevance system shares significant infrastructure with the recommendation pipeline, as Earlybird provides approximately 50% of the in-network candidate tweets for the For You timeline. The post also discusses real-time indexing of new tweets and how search quality is evaluated through both offline metrics and online A/B experiments.
unique_contribution: Describes the search relevance infrastructure that directly feeds candidate tweets into the recommendation pipeline via Earlybird.

title: Stability and Scalability for Search
abstract: This 2022 blog post describes improvements made to Twitter's search infrastructure for stability and scalability. Building on the Earlybird search index and blender architecture described in earlier posts, this update covers how Twitter addressed growing search traffic, improved fault tolerance, and reduced latency variability. The post likely discusses horizontal scaling strategies for Earlybird clusters, load balancing across search partitions, circuit breakers and degradation strategies for maintaining availability during traffic spikes, and how the search index handles the ever-growing volume of tweets. Given its 2022 date, it represents a relatively recent snapshot of the search infrastructure that serves as a major candidate source for the recommendation algorithm, providing roughly half of the in-network tweet candidates for the For You timeline.
unique_contribution: Provides an updated (2022) view of the search infrastructure's scaling challenges, relevant to understanding Earlybird's role in the recommendation pipeline.

title: Finatra 2.0: The Fast, Testable Scala Services Framework That Powers Twitter
abstract: This blog post announces Finatra 2.0, Twitter's open-source Scala HTTP and Thrift services framework built on top of TwitterServer and Finagle. Finatra provides a lightweight, testable framework for building microservices with dependency injection, routing, request filtering, and JSON serialization. Version 2.0 was a major rewrite featuring a new dependency injection framework (based on Google Guice), improved testability with embedded server testing, and better integration with Twitter's internal infrastructure. Finatra is the framework used to build many services in Twitter's recommendation pipeline, including components of Home Mixer, Product Mixer, and various candidate source services. The post covers the framework's architecture, its relationship to Finagle (Twitter's RPC system), how services are structured and tested, and performance characteristics. Understanding Finatra is useful for navigating the open-sourced recommendation algorithm codebase.
unique_contribution: Describes the services framework used to build the recommendation pipeline's microservices, useful for understanding the open-sourced codebase structure.

title: Twitter Experimentation: Technical Overview (DDG)
abstract: This blog post provides a technical overview of Twitter's experimentation platform, internally called DDG (Data-Driven Goodness or Detection and Diagnostics Gateway). The platform enables engineering teams to run A/B tests at scale to evaluate changes to the recommendation algorithm and other product features. The post covers the technical architecture including experiment assignment (how users are bucketed into control and treatment groups), metric computation pipeline, statistical analysis framework, and the UI for configuring and monitoring experiments. It discusses how Twitter handles experiment interactions (multiple concurrent experiments), the statistical methods used (including sequential testing and variance reduction techniques), and how experiment results feed back into algorithm development. DDG is critical to the recommendation system's evolution, as all changes to ranking models, candidate sources, and filtering rules are validated through controlled experiments.
unique_contribution: Describes the experimentation platform that validates all recommendation algorithm changes, essential context for understanding how Twitter iterates on its algorithms.

title: The What and Why of Product Experimentation at Twitter
abstract: This blog post provides a higher-level, product-focused complement to the DDG technical overview, explaining the philosophy and methodology behind Twitter's approach to product experimentation. It covers why Twitter uses A/B testing extensively, how experiments are designed and interpreted, the key metrics tracked (including engagement metrics, user retention, and content quality proxies), and the organizational processes around launching experiments. The post discusses common pitfalls in experimentation (novelty effects, metric gaming, p-hacking), Twitter's guardrail metrics that must not degrade, and how the experimentation culture influences product development decisions. For the recommendation system, this provides context on what metrics matter when evaluating algorithm changes and how the team balances engagement optimization with user experience quality.
unique_contribution: Provides the product philosophy behind Twitter's experimentation practices, offering context on which metrics guide recommendation algorithm improvements.

title: Detecting and Avoiding Bucket Imbalance in A/B Tests
abstract: This blog post addresses a specific statistical challenge in A/B testing at Twitter: detecting and preventing bucket imbalance, where treatment and control groups have systematically different user compositions. Such imbalance can lead to incorrect experiment conclusions, as observed differences may be due to population differences rather than the treatment effect. The post covers statistical methods for detecting imbalance (chi-squared tests, covariate checks), common causes at Twitter's scale (hash function collisions, triggering biases, time-of-day effects), and engineering solutions to prevent it (improved randomization, stratification, pre-experiment balance checks). This is relevant to the recommendation system because all ranking model changes are validated through A/B tests, and bucket imbalance could lead to launching inferior algorithm versions or rejecting genuine improvements.
unique_contribution: Addresses a specific statistical pitfall in large-scale A/B testing relevant to validating recommendation algorithm changes.

title: Introducing Birdwatch, a Community-Based Approach to Misinformation
abstract: This blog post introduces Birdwatch (later renamed Community Notes), Twitter's crowdsourced fact-checking program launched as a pilot in January 2021. The program allows participants to add context notes to tweets they believe are misleading, and to rate the helpfulness of notes written by others. The post describes the program's goals of combating misinformation through community input rather than centralized moderation, the initial pilot design with a limited set of contributors, and the transparency measures including publishing all notes and ratings data publicly. Birdwatch uses a matrix factorization-based algorithm to identify notes that are found helpful across users with diverse perspectives, specifically designed to surface notes that achieve consensus across political divides. The system represents a content moderation signal that interacts with the recommendation algorithm by potentially reducing the distribution of tweets with attached context notes.
unique_contribution: Introduces the Birdwatch/Community Notes system whose signals feed into content filtering and distribution decisions in the recommendation pipeline.

title: Helpful Birdwatch Notes Now Visible to Everyone on Twitter in the US
abstract: This blog post announces the expansion of Birdwatch (Community Notes) from a limited pilot to being visible to all Twitter users in the United States. The post describes the scaling of the program, the refinements made to the note-ranking algorithm based on pilot learnings, and the criteria for notes to become visible (requiring consensus across users with historically different rating patterns). It discusses how the bridging-based ranking algorithm was improved to better resist manipulation and coordinated rating campaigns. The expansion means Community Notes signals now actively influence what content users see in their feeds, as tweets with attached notes may be down-ranked by the recommendation algorithm. The post also covers the growth in contributor base and the volume of notes being written and rated.
unique_contribution: Documents the scaling of Community Notes to production, making its signals a real input to the recommendation algorithm's content filtering.

title: Introducing Our Crisis Misinformation Policy
abstract: This blog post introduces Twitter's crisis misinformation policy, which adds labels and reduces the algorithmic amplification of tweets containing false or misleading claims during crisis events such as armed conflicts, public health emergencies, and natural disasters. The policy defines what constitutes crisis misinformation, describes the labeling approach (adding context labels rather than removing content), and explains how labeled content is down-ranked in the recommendation algorithm to reduce its reach. This policy directly interfaces with the recommendation pipeline's filtering and ranking stages: tweets flagged under this policy receive visibility filtering that reduces their appearance in algorithmic surfaces like the For You timeline, search results, and explore. The post represents the policy layer that configures the trust and safety filtering components within the recommendation algorithm's final stages.
unique_contribution: Documents a specific content policy that configures the recommendation algorithm's trust-and-safety filtering layer to reduce distribution of crisis misinformation.

title: twitter/the-algorithm
abstract: The primary open-source repository containing Twitter/X's recommendation algorithm source code, released in March 2023 under the AGPL-3.0 license. The codebase is primarily Scala (66.4%), Java (19.7%), and Python (3.5%). It contains the full serving pipeline for content recommendations across all X product surfaces including the For You Timeline, Search, Explore, and Notifications. Key components include: Home Mixer (the main timeline serving orchestrator), Product Mixer (generic feed-building framework), SimClusters (community-based sparse embeddings for candidate retrieval), TwHIN (dense knowledge graph embeddings), RealGraph (user interaction prediction for in-network ranking), Earlybird (real-time tweet search index providing ~50% of in-network candidates), CR-Mixer (out-of-network candidate retrieval), GraphJet/UTEG (real-time graph-based recommendations), Navi (Rust-based ML model serving), Trust & Safety models (NSFW/abuse detection), Visibility Filtering, Follow Recommendations Service, Pushservice (notification ranking), and TweepCred (PageRank-based reputation). The architecture follows a pipeline: candidate sourcing from multiple sources, light ranking, heavy ranking via a ~48M parameter MaskNet model, then heuristic filtering and serving.
unique_contribution: The canonical open-source release of Twitter's complete recommendation algorithm, containing production code for all pipeline stages from candidate generation through serving.

title: twitter/the-algorithm-ml
abstract: The companion ML repository to twitter/the-algorithm, containing the Python/PyTorch model training code for Twitter's recommendation system, released under AGPL-3.0 in March 2023. The repository includes two primary model implementations: (1) the For You Heavy Ranker (located in projects/home/recap), which is the MaskNet-based ~48M parameter model that performs the final ranking of candidate tweets for the home timeline, and (2) TwHIN Embeddings (located in projects/twhin), based on the TwHIN paper (arxiv.org/abs/2202.05387) for learning dense embeddings of users and tweets in a heterogeneous information network. The codebase is designed for Linux with Nvidia GPU support, using torchrec for distributed training. The repository has a modular architecture with shared modules for common utilities, core abstractions, metrics, logging, optimizers, and data reading. While the training code is provided, the trained model weights and training data are not included, meaning the models cannot be directly reproduced without Twitter's proprietary datasets.
unique_contribution: Provides the actual training code for Twitter's heavy ranker (MaskNet) and TwHIN embedding models, the two most critical ML components of the recommendation pipeline.

title: twitter/GraphJet
abstract: GraphJet is a real-time graph processing library written in Java that maintains a full bipartite graph index over a sliding time window in memory on a single server. It powers Twitter's real-time recommendation systems by enabling collaborative filtering across rapidly evolving graph structures. Each server processes up to one million graph edges per second while concurrently computing up to 500 recommendations per second (several million edge reads/second). It uses compact edge encoding and a dynamic memory allocation scheme to balance rapid edge ingestion with concurrent query processing. The included demo implements two separate bipartite graphs: user-tweet interactions and tweet-hashtag associations, both queryable via REST endpoints. The library is the foundation for UTEG (User Tweet Entity Graph) recommendations in Twitter's For You timeline. Backed by a VLDB 2016 publication detailing the system internals.
unique_contribution: Demonstrates how Twitter maintains real-time bipartite graph indices in memory for collaborative filtering recommendations, achieving million-edge-per-second ingestion with concurrent serving.

title: twitter/communitynotes
abstract: Community Notes (formerly Birdwatch) is Twitter's crowdsourced fact-checking system. This repository contains the complete scoring algorithm code (Python 3.10) that determines which user-contributed notes are deemed helpful and displayed on potentially misleading posts. The core algorithm identifies notes that receive support from contributors with diverse perspectives, rather than only those from a single viewpoint, using a bridging-based approach. The repo includes the full scoring pipeline in the /scoring/src folder, daily published data (notes, ratings, contributor data), an AI Note Writer API for automated note generation, and comprehensive documentation. The system emphasizes reproducibility and transparency, with testing frameworks for assessing note quality impact. The codebase contains 725 commits with modules for scoring, evaluation, collaborative note generation, and data analysis.
unique_contribution: Complete open-source implementation of Twitter's crowdsourced fact-checking scoring algorithm, including the bridging-based approach that identifies cross-ideological consensus on note helpfulness.

title: twitter/cassovary
abstract: Cassovary is a JVM-based graph processing library written in Scala (98.3%) designed for space-efficient big graph analysis. A unidirectional graph with 10 million nodes and 1 billion edges consumes less than 6GB of memory, scaling linearly beyond that. The library prioritizes memory efficiency over flexibility, deliberately excluding database functionality and graph partitioning to allow complex algorithms to run on the graph efficiently. It includes common data structures and algorithms for graph mining and analysis, making it particularly suited for analyzing large social network graphs. Compatible with any JVM-hosted language. The repository contains 503 commits and is not archived, though development activity has slowed since 2015.
unique_contribution: Demonstrates Twitter's approach to space-efficient in-memory graph processing at scale, enabling complex graph algorithms on billion-edge social network graphs within practical memory constraints.

title: twitter/summingbird
abstract: Summingbird is a distributed computing library written in Scala that enables developers to write MapReduce programs using familiar collection transformation syntax (flatMap, sumByKey) and deploy identical code to batch processing (Scalding), real-time streaming (Storm), or hybrid batch/realtime architectures without modification. This lambda architecture abstraction allows a single codebase to handle both historical batch reprocessing and live streaming aggregation. The library supports pluggable storage backends (memcached, etc.) and provides fault-tolerant hybrid execution capabilities. Version 0.9.1 was published to Maven Central with artifacts for Scala 2.10 and 2.11. The repository was archived in January 2022 and marked as retired, containing 1,793 commits across its development history.
unique_contribution: Unified programming model for lambda architecture that lets the same code run in batch (Scalding) and real-time (Storm) modes, directly relevant to how Twitter computed features and aggregations for its recommendation pipeline.

title: twitter/pelikan
abstract: Pelikan is Twitter's unified cache backend framework for developing high-performance cache services, written primarily in C (61.3%) and Rust (34.3%). It replaced Twemcache as Twitter's primary caching solution. Key products include pelikan_segcache_rs (a Memcached-compatible server with exceptional memory efficiency backed by an NSDI 2021 paper), pelikan_pingserver_rs (a reference implementation for performance baselines), and momento_proxy (a Memcache-to-Momento compatibility layer). Core design principles include runtime separation of control and data planes, lockless data structures for predictably low latencies, modular composable configuration, multiple storage and protocol implementations, and built-in command logging for performance analysis. Requires Rust stable toolchain and LLVM/Clang 7.0+. Licensed under Apache 2.0.
unique_contribution: Twitter's next-generation caching framework with lockless data structures and segmented cache design (published at NSDI 2021), replacing Twemcache for production ML serving and recommendation infrastructure.

title: twitter/twemcache
abstract: Twemcache is Twitter's production-customized fork of Memcached 1.4.4, written in C (69.2%). It maintains full compatibility with the memcached ASCII protocol while introducing production-focused enhancements. Key features include TCP, UDP, and Unix domain socket support; memory organized into fixed-size slabs subdivided into equal-size items with configurable growth ratios; five pluggable eviction strategies (no eviction, per-class LRU, random slab eviction, least-recently-accessed and least-recently-created slab strategies) that can be stacked; lock-free stats collection with per-thread metrics aggregation; and a Klogger command logger. The project was archived in November 2021, superseded by Pelikan as Twitter's primary caching solution.
unique_contribution: Twitter's production memcached fork with multiple pluggable eviction strategies and lock-free metrics, historically used in the recommendation serving stack before being replaced by Pelikan.

title: twitter/finatra
abstract: Finatra is a lightweight Scala framework for building fast, testable applications on top of TwitterServer and Finagle. It provides a streamlined API for creating HTTP and Thrift servers with powerful feature and integration test support, Jackson-based JSON parsing with validation, optional Google Guice dependency injection (JSR-330 compliant), Logback MDC integration with com.twitter.util.Local for contextual logging, and SLF4J support. The framework is approximately 50 times faster than v1.6 in benchmarks and is used in production at Twitter and numerous organizations. It supports monthly releases via Maven Central with 2,661 commits across 92 releases under Apache 2.0 license. Finatra is the service framework underlying many of Twitter's recommendation system microservices.
unique_contribution: The Scala service framework underlying Twitter's recommendation microservices including Home Mixer, providing the HTTP/Thrift serving layer and dependency injection infrastructure.

title: twitter/distributedlog
abstract: Apache DistributedLog is a high-throughput, low-latency replicated log service originally developed at Twitter, later graduated from Apache Incubator (July 2017) and integrated as a sub-project of Apache BookKeeper. Written in Java (97.2%), it delivers millisecond-latency durable writes while handling concurrent logging from thousands of clients. Key features include strict message ordering across writers and readers, separation of stateless service tiers from stateful storage layers enabling independent scaling, optimization for multi-tenant datacenter environments (Mesos/Yarn), support for latency-sensitive OLTP applications, real-time stream processing, and analytics workloads. Provides efficient fan-in (writes) and fan-out (reads) operations with I/O isolation across many logs and tenants. Licensed under Apache 2.0.
unique_contribution: Twitter's replicated log service that provides the durable, ordered event streaming infrastructure underlying recommendation system data pipelines and real-time feature computation.

title: twitter-archive/gizzard
abstract: Gizzard is a middleware framework written in Scala (98.4%) for building fault-tolerant, distributed databases through custom sharding. It provides partitioning via a forwarding table mapping key ranges to specific partitions, replication through declarative replication trees with flexible topology configurations, fault tolerance by routing around failed replicas and buffering writes for later retry, migrations enabling elastic addition of machines without service interruption, and eventual consistency through idempotent/commutative write operations. It operates between clients and backend datastores, supporting any storage backend (SQL, Lucene, Redis, etc.). The repository is archived with 1,392 commits and 45 releases; Twitter is no longer maintaining this project. The last commit was 2019. Notable for 2.2k stars and 205 forks.
unique_contribution: Twitter's early distributed data sharding middleware that informed the design of later systems like Manhattan; demonstrates principles of elastic sharding, fault-tolerant writes, and eventual consistency that underpin Twitter's data infrastructure.

title: xai-org/x-algorithm
abstract: The x-algorithm repository contains the core recommendation system powering X's 'For You' feed as of 2026, representing the Grok-based replacement for Twitter's original 2023 open-sourced algorithm. The system combines two candidate sources: Thunder (in-network, an in-memory post store with real-time Kafka ingestion for sub-millisecond lookups of followed accounts' posts) and Phoenix (out-of-network, ML ranking using a transformer model with candidate isolation where posts cannot attend to each other). The system uses a Grok-based transformer model for ranking that eliminates hand-engineered features, relying entirely on transformer learning. It uses multi-action prediction (likes, replies, reposts, shares) rather than single relevance scores. Implements sophisticated filtering (duplicates, age, muted keywords, blocked authors). Built primarily in Rust (62.9%) with Python (37.1%). Home Mixer serves as the orchestration layer. Licensed under Apache 2.0. Has 16.3k stars and 2.8k forks.
unique_contribution: The successor to Twitter's 2023 open-sourced algorithm, showing the evolution to a Grok-based transformer that eliminates hand-engineered features and uses candidate isolation for out-of-network ranking.

title: Home Mixer README
abstract: Home Mixer is Twitter's primary service for constructing and serving home timelines, built on the Product Mixer framework. It powers three feeds: 'For You' (personalized recommendations), 'Following' (reverse chronological), and 'Lists' (member tweets). The For You pipeline flows through six stages: (1) Candidate Generation from Earlybird Search Index, User Tweet Entity Graph, CR-Mixer, and Follow Recommendations Service; (2) Feature Hydration assembling approximately 6,000 ranking features; (3) Scoring and Ranking via ML-based models; (4) Filtering and Heuristics applying author diversity, content balance (in-network vs out-of-network), deduplication, and visibility filtering; (5) Mixing integrating non-tweet content (ads, follow suggestions, prompts); (6) Product Features handling conversation modules, social context, pagination, and observability. The Following pipeline uses Earlybird for reverse-chronological delivery. Lists fetches from the timeline service. Both simpler pipelines include ads and recommendations.
unique_contribution: Definitive documentation of Twitter's home timeline serving pipeline, showing how 6,000 features are hydrated and how candidates from multiple sources (Earlybird, UTEG, CR-Mixer) are combined, ranked, filtered, and mixed with ads.

title: Product Mixer README
abstract: Product Mixer is Twitter's internal service framework and component library for building product surface areas and content delivery pipelines. It consists of three main elements: Core Libraries for building execution pipelines from reusable components with built-in monitoring, a Service Framework providing a common skeleton for deployment, and a Component Library of shared reusable components. The architecture operates through hierarchical pipelines: Product Pipelines route requests to Mixer or Recommendation Pipelines, which execute multiple Candidate Pipelines that fetch from sources, filter, decorate, and hydrate features. Mixer Pipelines combine heterogeneous candidate types (ads, tweets, users) while Recommendation Pipelines score and rank homogeneous candidates via Scoring Pipelines, marshalling results into domain and transport objects. Components are standardized, reusable, testable, and composable, with each having a well-defined abstraction. Pipelines function as configuration files specifying component usage and execution order.
unique_contribution: Documents Twitter's reusable pipeline framework that underlies Home Mixer and other product surfaces, showing the hierarchical pipeline architecture (Product -> Mixer/Recommendation -> Candidate) that standardizes all recommendation serving.

title: SimClusters v2 README
abstract: SimClusters is Twitter's general-purpose representation layer based on overlapping communities, encoding users and content as sparse, interpretable embedding vectors. The system models Twitter's follow graph as a bipartite producer-consumer graph. Community detection uses cosine similarity between follower sets with Metropolis-Hastings sampling-based clustering to identify approximately 145,000 communities across 20 million top producers, each producer affiliated with at most one community (KnownFor matrix V). Consumer embeddings (InterestedIn) are computed by multiplying the follow graph matrix by V. Producer embeddings use cosine similarity between follower patterns and community InterestedIn vectors. Tweet embeddings are initialized empty and updated in real-time as users favorite tweets, adding the favoriting user's InterestedIn vector. Topic embeddings derive from cosine similarity with time decay on topic-annotated tweets. Offline jobs (Scalding/GCP/BigQuery) generate KnownFor, InterestedIn, and producer embeddings. Real-time streaming (Heron/Storm) continuously updates tweet embeddings persisted to Manhattan/MemCache. This powers SimClusters ANN for personalized tweet recommendations.
unique_contribution: Complete documentation of SimClusters' embedding architecture including the 145K community detection algorithm, four types of embeddings (KnownFor, InterestedIn, Producer, Tweet), and the real-time tweet embedding update pipeline.

title: SimClusters ANN README
abstract: SimClusters ANN is Twitter's approximate nearest neighbor recommendation service that returns tweet candidates given a SimClusters embedding. It addresses the performance challenge of computing exact cosine similarity across large tweet corpora at Twitter's throughput demands (over 6,000 RPS). The five-step approximate algorithm: (1) Accept source SimCluster embedding with weighted cluster scores; (2) Fetch top 400 tweets from the top 50 SimClusters; (3) Build an approximate embedding matrix with limited cluster information; (4) Calculate dot products to identify top 200 candidate tweets; (5) Fetch complete embeddings for the 200 candidates and apply cosine similarity filtering with a threshold of 0.7 in production. This reduces I/O operations to 6% of what a naive approach would require (200 out of ~20,000 candidates). The service supports TWISTLY recommendations, interest-based suggestions, similar tweet discovery, and author-based recommendations.
unique_contribution: Detailed 5-step approximate cosine similarity algorithm with specific production parameters (top 50 clusters, 400 tweets, 200 candidates, 0.7 threshold) that enables efficient ANN retrieval at 6,000+ RPS using SimClusters embeddings.

title: CR-Mixer (Candidate Retrieval) README
abstract: CR-Mixer is Twitter's candidate generation service that acts as a lightweight coordinating layer delegating candidate generation tasks to underlying compute services. It operates through a 4-step pipeline: (1) Source Signal Extraction retrieving signals from external stores including UserProfileService and RealGraph; (2) Candidate Generation calling external candidate generation services; (3) Filtering applying deduplication and pre-ranking filters; (4) Ranking executing light ranking algorithms. The service provides a centralized platform for mixing multiple candidate sources, implements caching for result optimization, manages light ranking and common filtering layers, and includes version control for pipeline management, feature switch sets for co-owned configuration, and scribing/debugging/monitoring tools. Its strategic purpose is to reduce complexity in candidate generation while improving development velocity for faster testing and iteration across Twitter's personalization infrastructure.
unique_contribution: Documents the coordinating layer that unifies multiple candidate generation sources into a single service, showing the 4-step pipeline from signal extraction through light ranking that feeds into Home Mixer.

title: Timeline Ranker README
abstract: Timeline Ranker (TLR) is a legacy service that provides relevance-scored tweets from the Earlybird Search Index and User Tweet Entity Graph (UTEG) service. Despite its name, it performs minimal ranking itself, relying on pre-computed relevance scores rather than applying complex proprietary ranking models. It functions as a lightweight aggregation layer that fetches tweet candidates from two primary sources, applies basic ranking using existing scores, truncates results for downstream services, and filters/hydrates tweet data. It integrates with five major systems: Earlybird Search Index (ranked tweet candidates), UTEG (liked tweets from followed accounts), Social Graph Service (relationship data, blocks, mutes), TweetyPie (tweet field hydration for post-filtering), and Manhattan (user feature data like language preferences). Home Mixer calls Timeline Ranker to power both For You and Following timelines.
unique_contribution: Documents the legacy aggregation layer between Earlybird/UTEG and Home Mixer, revealing that despite its name, Timeline Ranker is primarily a fetch-and-filter service rather than a heavy ranking system.

title: UTEG (User Tweet Entity Graph) README
abstract: UTEG (User Tweet Entity Graph) is a Finagle thrift service built on the GraphJet framework that maintains user-tweet relationship data and serves recommendations through graph traversals. Its primary purpose is generating the 'XXX Liked' out-of-network tweet recommendations on Twitter's Home Timeline. The algorithm uses collaborative filtering: it accepts a user's weighted follow network as input, performs efficient graph traversal and weighted aggregation, and returns top-ranking tweets based on engagement frequency and follower weight. UTEG operates as a stateful service consuming Kafka streams to ingest and maintain data, with in-memory engagement records spanning 24-48 hours and automatic garbage collection of older events. It leverages GraphJet's high-performance in-memory storage engine for the underlying data operations.
unique_contribution: Documents the specific implementation of GraphJet-based collaborative filtering for out-of-network tweet discovery, showing how the 'Liked by people you follow' recommendations are generated with 24-48 hour engagement windows.

title: Earlybird Search README
abstract: Earlybird is Twitter's real-time search infrastructure built on Apache Lucene that indexes and queries billions of tweets with low latency. It serves two primary functions: Relevance Search (text search) and Timeline In-network Tweet retrieval (UserID-based search). The system divides the tweet index into three clusters: Realtime Cluster (public tweets from ~last 7 days), Protected Cluster (protected tweets from ~same timeframe), and Archive Cluster (all historical tweets up to ~2 days old). Each cluster is partitioned across multiple nodes using a distributed inverted index that is sharded and replicated. The indexing pipeline has Ingesters consuming tweets from Kafka topics, extracting fields and features, flowing to intermediate Kafka topics for Earlybird consumption. The serving phase has root nodes routing requests to appropriate clusters/partitions and merging responses. Uses a single-writer, multiple-reader structure for concurrent updates and queries. Primary use cases: tweet discovery (Top/Latest search), home timeline candidate generation (For You and Following), and notification serving.
unique_contribution: Documents Earlybird's three-cluster architecture (Realtime, Protected, Archive) and its dual role as both search engine and primary candidate source for the home timeline, including the Kafka-based indexing pipeline.

title: Earlybird Light Ranker README
abstract: The Earlybird Light Ranker is a logistic regression model predicting the likelihood of user engagement with a tweet, serving as a scalable pre-filter before the heavy ranker. Two variants exist: recap_earlybird (in-network tweets) and rectweet_earlybird (out-of-network/UTEG tweets), differing primarily in feature sets. Features come from four sources: (1) Index Ingester providing tweet metadata and static features (URL presence, cards, quotes, text quality scores from TweetTextScorer including offensiveness, entropy, shout factor, length, readability, plus Tweepcred author credibility); (2) Signal Ingester capturing real-time engagement metrics (retweet/favorite/reply counts) and spam signals via Heron topology; (3) User Table Features providing per-user attributes propagated to tweets via author lookup; (4) Search Context Features including UI language, content language preferences, and temporal context. Real-time features include health model outputs (pToxicity and pBlock scores). The documentation notes this is 'an old part of the stack' undergoing replacement, trained years ago with unconventional features.
unique_contribution: Detailed feature documentation for Twitter's pre-ranking logistic regression model, including the four feature sources (Index Ingester, Signal Ingester, User Table, Search Context) and specific features like TweetTextScorer quality metrics and Tweepcred.

title: Navi (ML Serving in Rust) README
abstract: Navi is Twitter's high-performance machine learning model serving server implemented in Rust, designed for production usage with emphasis on ultra-high performance, stability, and availability through minimalist design. It implements gRPC API compatibility with TensorFlow Serving, enabling seamless integration with existing TF Serving clients. The platform features a plugin architecture supporting different ML runtimes: TensorFlow (most mature, supporting multiple input tensor types), ONNX Runtime (supports single string input tensors, used in the home recommendation system with BatchPredictRequest formatting), and PyTorch (experimental, not production-grade). Key components include the core server, dr_transform (converts BatchPredictionRequest Thrift to ndarray format), segdense (configuration for feature value extraction), and thrift_bpr_adapter (generated Thrift code for BatchPredictionRequest handling). Source code excludes tests, benchmarks, and configs for security.
unique_contribution: Documents Twitter's custom Rust-based ML serving infrastructure that replaces TensorFlow Serving for production ranking model inference, including the plugin architecture for TF/ONNX/PyTorch runtimes and the BatchPredictRequest pipeline.

title: Trust & Safety Models README
abstract: This README identifies four machine learning models Twitter open-sourced for trust and safety: pNSFWMedia (detects tweets containing NSFW images including adult and porn content), pNSFWText (identifies tweets with NSFW text and adult/sexual topics), pToxicity (flags toxic tweets including marginal content like insults and certain types of harassment), and pAbuse (detects abusive content including violations of Twitter's terms of service such as hate speech, targeted harassment, and abusive behavior). The document provides minimal technical specifics, omitting model architectures, training methodologies, feature engineering, inference mechanisms, and performance metrics. Twitter explicitly noted they have several more models and rules not open-sourced due to the adversarial nature of trust and safety, acknowledging concerns about revealing proprietary safety mechanisms that could be exploited. The README serves as a high-level announcement rather than a technical reference.
unique_contribution: Identifies the four open-sourced T&S models (pNSFWMedia, pNSFWText, pToxicity, pAbuse) used in Twitter's recommendation filtering pipeline, though without technical implementation details.

title: Visibility Filtering (VisibilityLib) README
abstract: VisibilityLib is Twitter's centralized rule engine that instructs clients how to alter the display of certain content at read time, supporting legal compliance, product quality, user trust, and revenue protection.
