The system operates through interconnected components: SafetyLevel (represents product context like Timeline or Profile), Features (input data including safety labels, user metadata, relationships like block/follow status, and user-content associations like spam reports), Conditions (boolean evaluators combining to activate rules), Actions (responses ranging from hard filtering/content removal to soft filtering with labels/interstitials to ranking adjustments), Policy (ordered sequence of rules per SafetyLevel), RuleEngine (evaluates requests against policies), SafetyLabel (primary remediation mechanism associating safety concerns with tweets, users, DMs, media, and spaces), and SafetyLabelType (categorizes specific policy violations, though many are deprecated or experimental). The library was being reviewed and rebuilt at time of open-sourcing, with partial code removal and sanitized comments.
unique_contribution: Documents Twitter's centralized visibility rule engine architecture, showing how SafetyLevels, Conditions, and Actions compose into Policies that determine content display modifications across all product surfaces.

title: Follow Recommendations Service (FRS) README
abstract: The Follow Recommendations Service (FRS) is Twitter's recommendation engine for suggesting accounts to follow, powering Who-To-Follow (WTF) modules and FutureGraph tweet author recommendations. The system uses a modular five-stage pipeline: candidate generation (utilizing various user signals and algorithms), filtering (quality and health filters applied pre- and post-ranking), ranking (ML-based with features hydrated per user-candidate pair, scoring as a weighted sum of p(follow|recommendation) and p(positive engagement|follow)), transform (deduplication, social proof attachment, tracking tokens), and truncation. Each display location (e.g., post-NUX, ads) routes through a unique RecommendationFlow with tailored candidate sources and product configurations. ML predictions are served by a separate prediction service with offline-trained models.
unique_contribution: Describes Twitter's complete follow recommendation pipeline with its five-stage architecture and scoring formula combining follow probability with engagement probability.

title: Pushservice (Notifications) README
abstract: Pushservice is Twitter's primary notification recommendation engine responsible for generating and delivering personalized push notifications. It operates through two main handlers: RefreshForPushHandler, which determines whether a user should receive a recommendation-based notification by running a multi-stage pipeline (target construction/validation, candidate retrieval from multiple sources, data enrichment via batch backend requests, pre-ranking filtration, ranking with light and heavy stages, heavy filtration with iterative candidate evaluation, and delivery), and SendHandler, which manages delivery for pre-selected user-candidate pairs with validation, filtering, and multi-channel delivery (push and in-app). The architecture emphasizes a progressive filtering and ranking funnel that increases computational intensity at each stage to efficiently narrow candidates before final delivery.
unique_contribution: Documents Twitter's complete notification recommendation pipeline with its dual-handler architecture (RefreshForPush vs Send) and progressive filtering funnel design.

title: Notification Heavy Ranker README
abstract: The Notification Heavy Ranker is the core ranking model for Twitter's personalized notification recommendations, implemented as a multi-task learning system using the ClemNet architecture built on TensorFlow. It predicts probabilities that target users will open and engage with sent notifications. The model supports multiple variants through a pooling mechanism and includes configurable convolution layers. It operates as the third of four stages in the notification pipeline (candidate generation, light ranking, heavy ranking, quality control). The codebase includes feature engineering functions, continuous training and evaluation pipelines, checkpoint modification support for warm-start training, and hyperparameter management via centralized configuration. Specific hyperparameters, layer configurations, and feature definitions are not disclosed in the README.
unique_contribution: Identifies ClemNet as the architecture for Twitter's notification heavy ranker and describes its role in the four-stage notification pipeline.

title: TweepCred (PageRank Reputation) README
abstract: TweepCred computes user influence and reputation scores on Twitter using a weighted PageRank algorithm. The system operates in two stages: a preparation stage that constructs a graph with users as nodes and interactions (mentions, retweets) as edges, initializing each user's PageRank to a default value; and an iteration stage that repeatedly recalculates PageRank scores until convergence, measured by the total difference between successive iterations falling below a threshold. The algorithm incorporates mass prior (user reputation factors), random jump probability to prevent bias toward highly-connected nodes, and post-processing adjustments based on follower-to-following ratios. Final output includes raw PageRank scores and reputation scores scaled 0-100, with scores reduced for users exhibiting disproportionately high followings relative to followers, penalizing potentially inauthentic engagement patterns.
unique_contribution: Describes Twitter's user reputation scoring system based on weighted PageRank with follower-ratio adjustments to penalize inauthentic accounts.

title: Interaction Graph (RealGraph) README
abstract: The Interaction Graph, also known as RealGraph, consists of two components. Real Graph (bqe) builds a gradient boosting tree classifier to predict the likelihood of a Twitter user interacting with another user, creating labeled datasets from BigQuery tables representing directed edges between users with features like tweet counts, follows, and favorites. Real Graph (scio) performs daily aggregation of user interaction counts through multiple dataflow jobs, combining historical and current interaction data via rollup operations. The system tracks both public engagements (favorites, retweets, follows) and private engagements (profile views, tweet clicks, address book inclusion with user consent). Outputs include daily interaction type counts between user pairs, incoming interaction metrics, decayed sum aggregations of historical interactions, and ML-predicted interaction scores. These outputs inform recommendation ranking and personalization algorithms throughout the pipeline.
unique_contribution: Documents the dual-component RealGraph system (bqe for ML prediction, scio for daily aggregation) that powers user-to-user interaction scoring across Twitter's recommendation pipeline.

title: User Signal Service (USS) README
abstract: The User Signal Service (USS) is Twitter's centralized platform for aggregating and standardizing user interaction data. It collects explicit signals (favoriting, retweeting, replying) and implicit signals (tweet clicks, video views, profile visits, and additional behavioral metrics) from various underlying datasets and online services. USS normalizes this heterogeneous data into uniform formats that are utilized in two key downstream functions: candidate retrieval (helping identify potential tweets to show users) and machine learning feature generation for ranking stages. The README provides a high-level architectural overview of USS as a standardization layer but includes limited detail on specific data schemas, signal processing logic, or integration points with downstream services.
unique_contribution: Documents the centralized signal aggregation service that standardizes heterogeneous user interaction data for use across Twitter's recommendation pipeline.

title: TWML (Legacy ML Framework) README
abstract: TWML is one of Twitter's internal machine learning frameworks built on top of TensorFlow. The framework is largely deprecated and no longer actively maintained, with the codebase described as out of date and unused. However, it remains in the repository for historical context and continues to serve one active purpose: training Earlybird light ranking models used in Twitter's timeline system. The core component is the DataRecordTrainer class, which contains the central training logic for model training operations. The README provides minimal technical detail about specific features, supported model types, or architectural components, reflecting TWML's legacy status within Twitter's ML infrastructure ecosystem where it has been superseded by newer frameworks.
unique_contribution: Documents the legacy ML framework still used for training Earlybird light ranker models, providing context for understanding Twitter's ML infrastructure evolution.

title: Heavy Ranker (Recap/MaskNet) README
abstract: The Heavy Ranker uses a parallel MaskNet architecture that predicts probabilities for 10 types of user engagement with tweets: favorite, retweet, reply, profile click, video playback (50%+ watched), reply engagement by the tweet author, conversation clicks (standard and 2-minute duration), negative feedback, and report clicks. Predictions are combined via a weighted sum scoring formula where weights heavily emphasize author-engaged replies (weight 75.0) and strongly penalize reports (weight -369.0). The model outputs values between 0 and 1 for each engagement type. Training uses synthetic data generated via provided scripts since real training data cannot be released due to privacy restrictions. Configuration is managed through local_prod.yaml files. The README does not disclose model parameter count, layer configurations, or embedding dimensions, though the blog post describes it as approximately 48 million parameters.
unique_contribution: Provides the actual scoring formula weights for Twitter's heavy ranker, revealing how different engagement types are weighted (e.g., author replies at 75.0, reports at -369.0) to produce final tweet scores.

title: TwHIN Embeddings README
abstract: TwHIN (Twitter Heterogeneous Information Network) is a system for pretraining dense vector embedding features for Twitter entities. The embeddings are trained using graph-based relationships from three types of interaction patterns: user-to-user following relationships, user engagement with tweets (favorites), and user interactions with advertisements. Training expects parquet files with three columns representing graph edges (left-hand node, relation type, right-hand side node). The actual Twitter graph data is proprietary, but publicly available datasets are referenced for development. Within Twitter's infrastructure, these embeddings serve dual purposes: candidate retrieval for narrowing down potential content and as input features for recommender system ranking models. Development uses a containerized Docker workflow with shell scripts for building images and running experiments.
unique_contribution: Provides the training code and data format specification for Twitter's TwHIN entity embeddings that serve as features across multiple recommendation stages.

title: SimClusters: Community-Based Representations for Heterogeneous Recommendations at Twitter
abstract: SimClusters introduces a community-based representation framework for heterogeneous recommendations at Twitter, published at KDD 2020. The system discovers communities in the user-user follow graph and represents both users and content (tweets, events, topics) in a shared community embedding space. This enables recommendations across different entity types using a unified representation. SimClusters operates at massive scale, processing Twitter's full follow graph to identify thousands of overlapping communities. The community-based embeddings are used for multiple recommendation tasks including tweet recommendations, topic recommendations, and similar-user discovery. The approach balances representation quality with computational efficiency, enabling real-time candidate generation. SimClusters became one of the core out-of-network candidate sources in Twitter's recommendation pipeline.
unique_contribution: Introduces the community-based embedding framework that became one of Twitter's core out-of-network candidate generation systems, enabling cross-entity recommendations through shared community representations.

title: TwHIN: Embedding the Twitter Heterogeneous Information Network for Personalized Recommendation
abstract: TwHIN investigates knowledge-graph embeddings for entities in Twitter's heterogeneous information network, where nodes represent entities (users, content) and edges represent various interaction types. The key insight is that interactions from multiple relation types encode valuable information not fully captured by a single relation. The method learns dense embeddings by jointly modeling these diverse relations. Published at KDD 2022, the pretrained representations demonstrated improvements across multiple downstream tasks: personalized ads rankings, account follow-recommendation, offensive content detection, and search ranking. The paper also addresses practical deployment challenges including model compression techniques for latency reduction and handling parameter drift across model versions in production. Authors include El-Kishky, Markovich, Park, Verma, Kim, Eskander, Malkov, Portman, Samaniego, Xiao, and Haghighi.
unique_contribution: Introduces Twitter's heterogeneous information network embedding approach that jointly models multiple relation types and demonstrates production deployment across ads, follow recommendations, content safety, and search.

title: TwHIN-BERT: A Socially-Enriched Pre-trained Language Model for Multilingual Tweet Representations
abstract: TwHIN-BERT is a multilingual language model productionized at Twitter, trained on 7 billion tweets across 100+ languages. The key innovation is a dual training objective combining text-based self-supervision with a social objective that leverages engagement signals from Twitter's heterogeneous information network. This approach enriches language understanding with social context, producing representations that capture both semantic meaning and social dynamics. The model demonstrates significant improvements over established pre-trained language models on multilingual social recommendation and semantic understanding tasks. The authors (Zhang, Malkov, Florez, Park, McWilliams, Han, El-Kishky) open-sourced the model on HuggingFace along with curated benchmark datasets for hashtag prediction and social engagement tasks. Published at KDD 2023, TwHIN-BERT specifically targets the challenges of short, noisy user-generated social media content.
unique_contribution: Introduces a socially-enriched multilingual language model that combines text self-supervision with social engagement signals, trained on 7B tweets across 100+ languages and open-sourced on HuggingFace.

title: GraphJet: Real-Time Content Recommendations at Twitter
abstract: GraphJet is a real-time graph processing engine for content recommendations at Twitter, published at VLDB 2016. The system maintains a real-time bipartite interaction graph between users and tweets, enabling content-based recommendations through graph traversal algorithms such as SALSA (Stochastic Approach for Link-Structure Analysis). GraphJet is designed to handle Twitter's scale with billions of edges, providing low-latency graph operations for recommendation serving. The architecture supports incremental graph updates as new interactions arrive, maintaining a sliding window of recent interactions. GraphJet powers several recommendation features including tweet suggestions and the MagicRecs notification system. The system was designed as a successor to earlier graph-based approaches including Cassovary, offering improved real-time capabilities and memory efficiency for production deployment.
unique_contribution: Presents a purpose-built real-time graph engine for content recommendations that maintains a sliding-window bipartite interaction graph at Twitter scale, enabling low-latency SALSA-based recommendations.

title: WTF: The Who to Follow Service at Twitter
abstract: WTF (Who to Follow) describes Twitter's production account recommendation service, published at WWW 2013. The system generates personalized follow suggestions using a combination of graph-based algorithms operating on Twitter's social graph. Key approaches include random walks on the follow graph (particularly Personalized PageRank and circle of trust computations) to identify relevant accounts, combined with collaborative filtering techniques. The system processes Twitter's full social graph with hundreds of millions of users and billions of follow edges. Authors include Gupta, Goel, and colleagues from Twitter and Stanford. WTF was one of Twitter's earliest ML-powered recommendation systems and established the foundation for later follow recommendation services. The paper discusses both algorithmic approaches and production system design, including scalability challenges and serving architecture.
unique_contribution: Foundational paper describing Twitter's first production follow recommendation system using Personalized PageRank and circle-of-trust algorithms on the full social graph.

title: RealGraph: User Interaction Prediction at Twitter
abstract: RealGraph presents Twitter's system for predicting user-to-user interactions, published at the UEO Workshop in 2014. The system models pairwise interaction likelihood between Twitter users using features derived from historical engagement patterns including mentions, retweets, favorites, and profile visits. RealGraph builds a directed interaction graph where edges carry predicted interaction scores computed via machine learning models. These scores serve as key features throughout Twitter's recommendation pipeline, informing both candidate generation (identifying which users' content to surface) and ranking (determining content ordering in timelines). The paper by Kamath and Sharma describes the feature engineering approach and model design for predicting future interactions from historical engagement data. RealGraph became a foundational component of Twitter's in-network content recommendation system.
unique_contribution: Introduces the RealGraph interaction prediction system that became a foundational signal for Twitter's in-network content recommendations and timeline ranking.

title: Earlybird: Real-Time Search at Twitter
abstract: Earlybird is Twitter's real-time search engine designed to index and serve search queries over the full stream of tweets, published at IEEE ICDE 2012. The system addresses the unique challenges of real-time search: extremely high ingestion rates (thousands of tweets per second), the need for immediate availability of new content in search results, and query patterns that favor recency. Earlybird uses an inverted index architecture optimized for append-heavy workloads with a novel single-writer, lock-free concurrent read design that enables indexing without blocking query serving. The system partitions the tweet corpus temporally, with newer segments held in memory for low-latency access. Authors include Busch, Gade, and others from Twitter. Earlybird serves as the retrieval backend for both user-facing search and internal candidate retrieval in the recommendation pipeline, functioning as a light ranker for in-network tweet candidate generation.
unique_contribution: Describes the real-time search engine architecture with lock-free concurrent indexing that serves as both Twitter's search backend and in-network candidate retrieval system for recommendations.

title: kNN-Embed: Locally Smoothed Embedding Mixtures For Multi-interest Candidate Retrieval
abstract: kNN-Embed addresses the limitation that standard nearest-neighbor search produces low-diversity results for multi-interest users, published at PAKDD 2023. The method represents each user as a smoothed mixture over learned item clusters representing distinct interests, then queries each interest component proportionally to retrieve candidates spanning the user's varied preferences. This approach ensures candidate retrieval reflects the full breadth of user interests rather than converging on a single dominant preference. The authors (El-Kishky, Markovich, Leung, Portman, Haghighi, Xiao) demonstrate significant improvements in overall recall and improved diversity across three datasets compared to conventional approximate nearest neighbor retrieval. A notable contribution is the release of a large Twitter follow-graph dataset to facilitate future research in graph-mining and recommendation systems.
unique_contribution: Introduces a multi-interest embedding mixture approach for diverse candidate retrieval that outperforms standard kNN, with an accompanying open-source Twitter follow-graph dataset.

title: MiCRO: Multi-interest Candidate Retrieval Online
abstract: MiCRO presents a generative statistical framework for personalized recommendations in ephemeral environments like social media, addressing three key challenges: understanding appeal for frequently-created items, adapting to temporal shifts in relevance, and modeling users' diverse interests. The method models multi-interest user preferences alongside temporal representations of items, specifically engineered to accommodate both new items and evolving engagement patterns. Authors Portman, Ragain, and El-Kishky demonstrate strong empirical performance on candidate retrieval tasks using two large-scale datasets: an open-source temporal user-to-user follow interaction dataset and a temporal user-tweet favorite interaction dataset contributed as a new open-source resource. The work bridges recommendation systems with temporal dynamics and multi-faceted user interest modeling for real-time candidate generation.
unique_contribution: Introduces a generative framework for multi-interest candidate retrieval that explicitly models temporal dynamics of ephemeral social media content, with a contributed open-source tweet interaction dataset.

title: TwERC: High Performance Ensembled Candidate Generation for Ads Recommendation
abstract: TwERC presents a machine learning-first heterogeneous re-architecture for candidate generation in Twitter's large-scale advertisement recommendation system, submitted to AdKDD 2023. The approach combines a real-time light ranker with two complementary sourcing strategies: a graph-based strategy leveraging similarity in the interaction graph (yielding a 4.08% revenue gain) and a rankscore-based strategy that caches previous scores from the ranking stage (yielding a 1.38% revenue gain). The authors (Cai, Prabakar, Serrano Rebuelta, Rosen, Monti, Janocha, Lazovich, Raj, Shrinivasan, Li, Markovich) demonstrate that these strategies have complementary biases that benefit both the light ranker and each other when ensembled. The paper also proposes metrics for evaluating product trade-offs in industrial candidate generation systems, addressing practical deployment considerations.
unique_contribution: Describes Twitter's production ads candidate generation re-architecture combining graph-based and rankscore-based strategies with quantified revenue improvements (4.08% and 1.38% respectively).

title: Click-through Prediction for Advertising in Twitter Timeline
abstract: This KDD 2015 paper by Li, Lu, Mei and colleagues at Twitter describes the click-through rate prediction system for advertising in the Twitter timeline. The work addresses the unique challenges of predicting ad engagement in a social media feed context, where ads compete with organic content for user attention. The paper presents the feature engineering approach, model architecture, and production system design for Twitter's ads ranking pipeline. It covers the specific challenges of sparse and noisy engagement signals in timeline advertising, real-time serving requirements, and the interplay between organic content and promotional content in the same feed. The system was one of Twitter's early ML-driven ad ranking approaches that laid groundwork for later systems.
unique_contribution: Early description of Twitter's production ad CTR prediction system for timeline advertising, addressing the unique challenge of ads competing with organic social content.

title: Addressing Delayed Feedback for Continuous Training with Neural Networks in CTR Prediction
abstract: This RecSys 2019 paper by Ktena, Tejani, Theis, Myana, Dilipkumar, Huszar, Yoo, and Shi from Twitter addresses the critical challenge of delayed positive labels (conversions) in display advertising CTR prediction systems. When training continuously, treating unlabeled data as negative underestimates click-through rates, harming both user experience and advertiser performance. The research evaluates 5 different loss functions (3 applied to this problem for the first time) alongside shallow and deep neural network architectures, tested on both public and proprietary datasets (668 million data points). Key results include a 3% relative cross-entropy improvement over prior state-of-the-art in offline experiments and a 55% gain in revenue per thousand requests against naive log loss in online production experiments. The paper also analyzes engineering implementation costs for each approach in real production systems.
unique_contribution: Systematically evaluates loss functions for handling delayed feedback in continuous CTR model training, demonstrating 55% revenue improvement in production with practical engineering cost analysis.

title: MaskNet: Introducing Feature-Wise Multiplication to CTR Ranking Models by Instance-Guided Mask
abstract: MaskNet proposes introducing multiplicative feature interactions to deep CTR ranking models through a novel MaskBlock building unit, published at DLP-KDD 2021. The MaskBlock integrates layer normalization, instance-guided masking, and feed-forward operations, where the instance-guided mask performs element-wise product on both feature embeddings and feed-forward layers guided by the input instance. This approach augments the typical additive-only operations in deep ranking networks with multiplicative interactions that more effectively capture complex feature cross patterns. Authors Wang, She, and Zhang demonstrate that MaskNet models outperform state-of-the-art models such as DeepFM and xDeepFM significantly across three real-world datasets. This architecture was adopted by Twitter as the basis for their approximately 48-million-parameter heavy ranker used in the home timeline recommendation pipeline, making it the core ranking architecture in the open-sourced algorithm.
unique_contribution: Introduces the MaskBlock architecture with instance-guided multiplicative feature interactions that became the foundation of Twitter's production heavy ranker for home timeline ranking.

title: Algorithmic Amplification of Politics on Twitter
abstract: This landmark study by Twitter researchers (Huszar, Ktena, O'Brien, Belli et al.) conducted a large-scale randomized controlled experiment to measure how Twitter's algorithmic personalization affects political content visibility. Nearly 2 million daily active accounts were assigned to a reverse-chronological feed without algorithmic personalization, creating a control group against the standard algorithmic timeline. The study examined tweets from elected legislators across 7 countries and U.S. news media sources. Key findings: in 6 of 7 countries, mainstream political right enjoyed higher algorithmic amplification than the mainstream political left. Right-leaning news sources in the U.S. also received greater algorithmic promotion. Contrary to common assumptions, the algorithm did not disproportionately amplify far-left or far-right extremist content compared to moderate viewpoints. The paper contributes to evidence-based debate about how personalization algorithms shape political content consumption patterns, providing one of the first insider analyses of algorithmic political amplification at scale.
unique_contribution: First large-scale RCT by Twitter engineers measuring political amplification across 7 countries, showing systematic right-leaning amplification in most countries studied.

title: The 2021 RecSys Challenge Dataset: Fairness is not Optional
abstract: This paper by Belli et al. describes the dataset released for the 2021 ACM RecSys Challenge, which was based on Twitter's tweet engagement prediction task. The dataset is designed to encourage participants to consider fairness alongside traditional engagement metrics. It provides real Twitter interaction data for predicting user engagement with tweets (likes, replies, retweets, quote tweets). The paper argues that fairness considerations should be an integral part of recommender system evaluation, not an afterthought. It discusses how recommendation systems can introduce or amplify biases and proposes that challenge participants should optimize for both engagement prediction accuracy and fairness-aware metrics. The dataset includes features about tweets, users, and their interactions, making it one of the largest publicly available Twitter recommendation datasets. This work reflects Twitter's internal efforts to incorporate responsible ML practices into their recommendation pipeline.
unique_contribution: Official Twitter dataset for engagement prediction with explicit fairness requirements, providing real interaction data for the research community.

title: From Optimizing Engagement to Measuring Value
abstract: This paper by Milli, Belli, and Hardt (FAccT 2021) identifies a critical disconnect in recommendation systems: engagement metrics like clicks and dwell time do not necessarily align with what users or platforms truly value. The authors argue there is potentially a large gap between engagement signals and a desired notion of 'value' worth optimizing for. Rather than continuing to optimize engagement proxies, they propose using measurement theory to help system designers confront their actual values explicitly, build latent variable models that directly optimize for 'value' instead of engagement proxies, and evaluate whether their operationalization genuinely captures intended outcomes. The framework was tested at Twitter with millions of users, conducting qualitative assessments to validate whether the model actually measures the kind of value stakeholders care about. This represents a philosophical shift from assuming clicks equal success to deliberately defining and measuring what success should mean in recommender systems.
unique_contribution: Proposes measurement theory framework to move beyond engagement optimization toward explicit value measurement, validated at Twitter scale with millions of users.

title: Birdwatch: Crowd Wisdom and Bridging Algorithms
abstract: This paper describes the algorithmic foundation of Twitter's Birdwatch (now Community Notes) system for crowdsourced misinformation annotation. The authors developed a matrix factorization (MF) based approach to identify annotations that appeal broadly across heterogeneous user groups, referred to as 'bridging-based ranking.' Rather than selecting the most popular annotations overall, the algorithm identifies contributions that resonate with diverse user communities spanning ideological divides. The research demonstrated that algorithmically-selected annotations outperformed both average annotations and crowd-generated baselines. In a randomized Twitter experiment, people who saw annotations selected through the bridging-based approach were significantly less likely to reshare social media posts containing misinformation. The study combined data from the online annotation platform where contributors both flag misinformation and rate others' contributions, paired with real-world impact measurement. This work provides evidence that thoughtfully-designed algorithms incorporating collective intelligence can effectively combat misinformation.
unique_contribution: Describes the bridging-based matrix factorization algorithm behind Community Notes, with experimental evidence that it reduces misinformation resharing.

title: Evaluating Twitter's Algorithmic Amplification of Low-Credibility Content
abstract: This study by Shao et al. (EPJ Data Science 2024) uses an observational approach to analyze algorithmic amplification of low-credibility content on Twitter over a 14-day period in January 2023. The researchers analyzed an original dataset of 2.7 million posts on COVID-19 and climate change, identifying tweets from low-credibility domains and comparing impression generation between low and high-credibility content. Using a bootstrapping model with stratifications based on engagement levels and follower counts, plus additional variables examining toxicity, political bias, and verification status, the study found that tweets containing low-credibility URL domains perform significantly better than tweets that do not across both datasets. Additional patterns emerged: high-toxicity tweets, right-leaning content, and low-credibility tweets from verified accounts all received heightened algorithmic visibility. The authors conclude that Twitter's recommender system may have facilitated the diffusion of false content, even when originating from notoriously low-credibility sources.
unique_contribution: Empirical evidence that Twitter's algorithm amplifies low-credibility content more than high-credibility content, using 2.7M posts on COVID-19 and climate change.

title: Crowdsourced Audit of Twitter's Recommender Systems
abstract: This study by Bouchaud, Chavalarias, and Panahi, published in Scientific Reports (2023), presents a crowdsourced methodology for auditing Twitter's recommendation algorithms. The researchers recruited participants to collect data about their Twitter experience, enabling external analysis of how the platform's algorithms curate content. The audit examines multiple aspects of Twitter's recommender systems including content ranking, user recommendations, and timeline curation. By aggregating data from many users with diverse profiles and usage patterns, the study provides an outside-in view of algorithmic behavior that complements internal analyses. The methodology addresses the challenge of auditing opaque algorithmic systems by leveraging collective user observations. Findings contribute to understanding how Twitter's algorithms shape information exposure and user experience, with implications for platform transparency and accountability.
unique_contribution: Novel crowdsourced audit methodology for externally examining Twitter's recommendation algorithms through collective user data collection.

title: Echo Chambers in the Age of Algorithms: An Audit of Twitter's Friend Recommender System
abstract: This study by Duskin, Schafer, West, and Spiro (ACM Web Science 2024) conducted an empirical audit of Twitter's Who-To-Follow recommendation system during the 2022 U.S. midterm elections. Researchers created automated accounts following left and right-aligned politicians, then grew networks using the platform's recommender algorithm. This was paired with an observational study of actual Twitter users following the same politicians. Key findings reveal a nuanced picture: the algorithm led accounts into dense and reciprocal neighborhoods that structurally resemble echo chambers. However, algorithmic recommendations produced less political homogeneity compared to accounts growing their networks through social endorsement. Accounts following only algorithmic recommendations had fewer opportunities to encounter content centered on false or misleading election narratives compared to choosing friends based on social endorsement. Rather than simply amplifying echo chambers, Twitter's recommender system appears more complex -- creating structurally similar patterns while potentially reducing exposure to misinformation.
unique_contribution: Empirical evidence that Twitter's Who-To-Follow creates structural echo chambers but actually produces less political homogeneity and less misinformation exposure than organic network growth.

title: Revealing The Secret Power: How Algorithms Can Influence Content Visibility on Twitter/X
abstract: This study by Conti, De Cristofaro et al. (NDSS 2026) analyzed over 40 million tweets from two datasets: the Ukraine-Russia conflict (17M+ tweets) and the 2024 US Elections (35M tweets). The authors introduced a 'p-score' metric -- view count divided by number of followers -- to normalize visibility by author popularity. Analysis occurred at content, user, and network levels. At the content level, the algorithm systematically penalizes external links: tweets without URLs received 8x higher median p-scores for Ukraine-Russia discussions and 4x for US Elections, regardless of source credibility or political bias. No systematic penalization of content based on political stance or factuality was found. At the user level, individual accounts experienced stark disparities (e.g., RT.com vs The Kyiv Independent showed a tenfold difference). At the network level, no evidence emerged of systematic community-level penalization based on ideology. The key finding is that Twitter/X's algorithm favors native content over link-sharing but shows little evidence of ideology-driven suppression.
unique_contribution: Introduces p-score metric for normalized visibility analysis; demonstrates URL penalization is the dominant algorithmic effect rather than ideological bias, using 40M+ tweets.

title: Auditing Political Exposure Bias: Algorithmic Amplification on Twitter/X During the 2024 U.S. Presidential Election
abstract: This study (ACM FAccT 2025) deployed 120 artificial accounts across four political groups -- left-leaning, right-leaning, balanced, and neutral -- collecting approximately 9.79 million tweets over six weeks during October-November 2024. Each account type was configured to follow specific media outlets and political figures aligned with their designated orientation, with timelines collected four times daily. Key metrics included weighted occurrence per 1,000 tweets and the Gini coefficient for exposure inequality. Findings show significant inequality in content distribution across all user groups, with right-leaning accounts experiencing the highest exposure inequality. Partisan accounts encountered amplified exposure to ideologically aligned content while experiencing reduced visibility of opposing viewpoints. Left-leaning users saw left-aligned voices amplified over 36% above baseline, with similar patterns for conservative users. Notably, neutral accounts (following nobody) revealed a default right-leaning bias in their default timelines. The algorithm reinforced existing political preferences rather than promoting diverse perspectives.
unique_contribution: Large-scale sock-puppet audit during 2024 US election showing default right-leaning bias for neutral accounts and algorithmic reinforcement of political preferences.

title: Engagement, User Satisfaction, and the Amplification of Divisive Content on Social Media
abstract: This pre-registered algorithmic audit examines Twitter's engagement-based ranking algorithm against a reverse-chronological baseline. The researchers discovered that engagement-driven ranking amplifies emotionally charged, out-group hostile content that users report makes them feel worse about their political out-group. Notably, users did not actually prefer the posts selected by the engagement-optimizing algorithm, indicating a fundamental mismatch between engagement metrics and user preferences. The study further tested an alternative ranking system based on stated user preferences, which reduced hostile and partisan content but potentially reinforced pro-attitudinal material. The methodology involved gathering user feedback on political tweets and measuring emotional responses alongside content characteristics, comparing Twitter's engagement-based ranking, a reverse-chronological baseline, and a preference-based ranking alternative. The authors conclude that there is a necessity for a more nuanced approach to content ranking that balances engagement and users' stated preferences, highlighting a fundamental tension in social media design where engagement optimization may actively harm user satisfaction.
unique_contribution: Pre-registered audit demonstrating that Twitter's engagement optimization amplifies divisive content that users do not actually prefer, revealing engagement-satisfaction mismatch.

title: Lower Quantity, Higher Quality: Auditing News Content on Twitter/X Algorithmic vs Chronological Timelines
abstract: This sociotechnical audit recruited 243 U.S. Twitter/X users via Prolific and deployed a custom browser extension to track content exposure across three phases: an observation week of passive monitoring, then two intervention weeks randomly assigning users to algorithmic ('For You') or chronological ('Following') timelines, then switching conditions. Over 800,000 tweets were collected along with surveys measuring user perceptions. The algorithmic timeline showed a surprising moderating effect on news content: users saw news sources that were significantly less ideologically congruent, less extreme, and marginally more reliable. However, the algorithmic timeline dramatically deprioritized news overall: chronological feed showed 3.26% news density vs 0.94% for algorithmic. Users saw substantially fewer tweets with external news links in the algorithmic condition, though engagement with non-news content increased. Despite behavioral changes, user attitudes about platform bias and news trustworthiness did not shift significantly across conditions, revealing a perception gap.
unique_contribution: Field experiment showing algorithmic timeline reduces news quantity by 70% but improves remaining news quality (less extreme, more reliable), with a notable perception gap among users.

title: Future Challenges for Online, Crowdsourced Content Moderation: Evidence from Twitter's Community Notes
abstract: Published in the Journal of Online Trust and Safety, this paper examines the future challenges facing Twitter's Community Notes (formerly Birdwatch) crowdsourced content moderation system. The paper analyzes how the Community Notes system works in practice, evaluating its effectiveness at scale and identifying structural challenges that could undermine its long-term viability. Topics likely covered include the bridging algorithm's ability to find cross-partisan consensus, scalability challenges as the platform grows, potential for gaming or manipulation of the crowdsourced rating system, coverage gaps where notes are needed but not provided, and the tension between speed of note creation and the pace of viral misinformation spread. The paper contributes to the broader literature on platform governance and algorithmic content moderation by providing empirical evidence from one of the largest crowdsourced fact-checking experiments deployed on a major social media platform.
unique_contribution: Analysis of structural challenges facing Community Notes at scale, including gaming risks, coverage gaps, and speed-of-response limitations.

title: Twitter Heron: Stream Processing at Scale
abstract: This ACM SIGMOD 2015 paper describes Heron, Twitter's real-time stream processing engine built as a successor to Apache Storm. Heron was designed to address Storm's limitations in scalability, debuggability, manageability, and efficient resource usage. The system processes billions of events per day for use cases including real-time analytics, continuous computation, and online machine learning at Twitter. Key architectural decisions include a process-based topology execution model (replacing Storm's thread-based approach), explicit back-pressure mechanisms to handle slow consumers without data loss, and a modular architecture separating the logical and physical plans. Heron maintains API compatibility with Storm, allowing seamless migration of existing topologies. The system achieves significant throughput improvements and reduced latency compared to Storm, while providing better isolation between topology components and easier debugging through standard profiling tools. Heron became the foundation for Twitter's real-time data processing infrastructure supporting the recommendation pipeline.
unique_contribution: Describes Twitter's production stream processing engine replacing Storm, with process-based execution model and back-pressure mechanisms for reliable real-time processing.

title: Twitter Heron: Towards Extensible Streaming Engines
abstract: This ICDE 2017 paper extends the original Heron work, focusing on making the streaming engine more extensible and adaptable to different deployment environments and workload requirements. Published in collaboration with Microsoft Research, the paper describes architectural enhancements that allow Heron to support multiple scheduling backends, state management strategies, and processing semantics. The extensibility framework enables operators to swap components such as the scheduler (supporting YARN, Mesos, Aurora, Kubernetes), the state manager (ZooKeeper, file-based), and the metrics collection system. The paper also discusses performance optimizations and benchmarks comparing Heron against other streaming frameworks. This work reflects the evolution of Heron from a Twitter-internal system to an open-source project (Apache Heron) designed for broader community adoption with pluggable components for different operational requirements.
unique_contribution: Describes Heron's extensibility framework with pluggable schedulers, state managers, and processing semantics for adapting to diverse deployment environments.

title: Summingbird: A Framework for Integrating Batch and Online MapReduce
abstract: This VLDB 2014 paper describes Summingbird, Twitter's open-source framework that allows developers to write MapReduce programs that can run in both batch (Hadoop/Scalding) and online (Storm/Heron) modes without code changes. The framework addresses the lambda architecture challenge of maintaining separate codebases for batch and streaming computations. Summingbird provides a unified API based on monoid algebra, where computations are expressed as compositions of map, flatMap, and sum operations over monoids. This algebraic approach guarantees that batch and online results are mathematically equivalent. The system handles the complexity of merging batch and real-time results transparently. At Twitter, Summingbird powers various aggregation workloads including trending topics computation, engagement counting, and feature generation for ML models. The framework significantly reduces development time by eliminating the need to maintain parallel batch and streaming implementations.
unique_contribution: Unified batch-streaming framework using monoid algebra to guarantee equivalent results, powering Twitter's feature aggregation and trending topics.

title: Robust, Scalable, Real-Time Event Time Series Aggregation at Twitter (TSAR)
abstract: This ACM SIGMOD 2018 paper describes TSAR (TimeSeries AggregatoR), Twitter's system for real-time event time series aggregation. TSAR addresses the challenge of computing aggregate metrics over event streams at Twitter's scale, where billions of events per day must be processed with low latency. The system provides robust aggregation with exactly-once semantics despite node failures and network partitions. Key design decisions include event-time processing (as opposed to processing-time), watermark-based windowing for handling late-arriving data, and incremental aggregation with checkpointing for fault tolerance. TSAR supports multiple aggregation functions and time granularities, enabling use cases such as real-time engagement metrics, trending topic detection, and ML feature computation. The system integrates with Twitter's broader data infrastructure including Heron for stream processing and Manhattan for storage, providing the real-time aggregation layer that feeds into the recommendation system's feature pipeline.
unique_contribution: Production real-time aggregation system with exactly-once semantics for event-time processing at Twitter scale, feeding ML feature pipelines.

title: The Unified Logging Infrastructure for Data Analytics at Twitter
abstract: This VLDB 2012 paper describes Twitter's production logging system that evolved from application-specific logging approaches to a standardized unified format. The system transitioned to a 'client events' log format using common, well-formatted, flexible Thrift messages. A notable design feature involves pre-materializing 'session sequences' -- compact data summaries that enable rapid querying of common analytical patterns without scanning raw logs. The approach treats user sessions as the fundamental analytical unit, which shaped how data gets aggregated and stored, significantly improving the organization's capacity for rapid experimentation and iteration. The unified infrastructure streamlined log collection and data analysis across the organization. This logging infrastructure is foundational to Twitter's recommendation system, as it provides the engagement signals (clicks, likes, retweets, dwell time) that serve as training labels and evaluation metrics for the ML models in the ranking pipeline.
unique_contribution: Describes Twitter's unified logging format using Thrift messages and session materialization, enabling rapid experimentation that underpins all ML training data collection.

title: A Large Scale Analysis of Hundreds of In-Memory Cache Clusters at Twitter
abstract: This USENIX OSDI 2020 paper presents a comprehensive analysis of Twitter's in-memory caching infrastructure, examining hundreds of cache clusters comprising tens of thousands of servers. The study analyzes workload patterns, cache efficiency, and resource utilization across Twitter's diverse caching use cases including timeline serving, user profile lookups, and ML feature retrieval. Key findings include characterization of cache access patterns (temporal locality, key-size distributions, hit rates), identification of inefficiencies in cache memory allocation, and recommendations for improved caching strategies. The analysis reveals that different workload types (e.g., timeline caching vs. social graph lookups) exhibit fundamentally different access patterns requiring tailored caching policies. The paper provides insights into how caching supports low-latency serving in the recommendation pipeline, where cached features and precomputed scores enable the system to serve personalized timelines within the sub-1.5-second latency budget.
unique_contribution: Largest published analysis of production caching infrastructure, revealing workload-specific access patterns across hundreds of Twitter cache clusters.

title: How Twitter Built a Massive ML Pipeline Using Kafka
abstract: This Confluent blog post describes how Twitter built a streaming data logging system using Apache Kafka and Kafka Streams to replace their offline batch ML pipeline, handling billions of daily tweets with thousands of features per tweet. The system uses a customized left-join DSL built on Kafka Streams' Processor API rather than native join functionality. It employs three state store types: KeySortedStore (WindowStore) for records keyed by payload/timestamp/sequence, TimeSortedStore (KeyValueStore) maintaining records sorted by timestamp and offset to preserve serving order, and JoinedIndicatorStore tracking successfully matched records. The pipeline performs feature-label joins, the predominant operation in ML logging systems. The system reduced latency from seven days to approximately one day while improving model quality. The pipeline intentionally disables strict fault tolerance, accepting moderate data loss since ML models assume i.i.d. distribution of examples. Records are emitted in serving order to prevent feature distribution distortion from delayed delivery.
unique_contribution: Detailed description of Twitter's Kafka-based ML training pipeline with custom left-join DSL, reducing training data latency from 7 days to 1 day.

title: Ranking Tweets with TensorFlow
abstract: This TensorFlow blog post describes Twitter's migration from Lua Torch to TensorFlow for their home timeline ranking system, which scores thousands of tweets per user. Twitter implemented a 'split network' architecture that processes feature groups separately -- user features, author features, and tweet features -- with each split learning a compact representation before concatenation. This architecture improved training speed by approximately 22% while maintaining or improving model quality. The ranking model processes thousands of features across three entity types: tweet (recency, media presence, engagement metrics), author (relationship history, connection strength), and user (past engagement patterns, usage frequency). TensorFlow's warm start capability allowed model retraining using previous weights as initialization, requiring only 20% of training examples for equivalent quality while enabling frequent model refreshes for real-time adaptation. TensorBoard revealed training behaviors, the Profiler identified performance bottlenecks, and Model Analysis enabled dataset segmentation analysis. This is a key document for understanding the pre-MaskNet ranking architecture.
unique_contribution: Describes Twitter's split network ranking architecture in TensorFlow, including warm-start training requiring only 20% of examples and 22% faster training.

title: Finagle (RPC system)
abstract: Finagle is Twitter's extensible RPC (Remote Procedure Call) framework for the JVM, implementing uniform client and server APIs for several protocols. Written in Scala, it provides idiomatic APIs for both Scala and Java developers. Finagle is designed for high performance and concurrency, enabling developers to construct servers that handle many simultaneous connections. The framework is protocol-agnostic, with most of its codebase being protocol-independent, which simplifies implementing new protocols. Supported protocols include HTTP, Thrift, Postgres, and others. Finagle is a foundational component of Twitter's service infrastructure -- the Home Mixer serving layer, candidate generation services, and ranking services all communicate via Finagle RPC calls. The framework handles connection pooling, load balancing, failure detection, and retry logic transparently. Understanding Finagle is essential for comprehending how the various components of Twitter's recommendation pipeline communicate in the distributed system architecture.
unique_contribution: Twitter's foundational RPC framework enabling inter-service communication across the entire recommendation pipeline.

title: Finatra (service framework)
abstract: Finatra is Twitter's Scala services framework built on top of TwitterServer and Finagle, providing a higher-level abstraction for building HTTP and Thrift-based services. The framework represents a complete redesign from version 1.x, offering modern tooling for constructing scalable services. Finatra enables developers to define servers and controllers that function as service abstractions to manage API endpoints, compose filters at multiple levels (individual controllers, specific routes, or entire server), and build both HTTP and Thrift-based services through dedicated modules. The framework provides utilities for logging, dependency injection, statistics collection, JSON handling, case class validation, and testing infrastructure including the EmbeddedTwitterServer for verifying service behavior. Finatra is used throughout Twitter's recommendation infrastructure to build the microservices that implement candidate generation, ranking, and serving components. The Home Mixer and other recommendation services are built using Finatra's service abstractions.
unique_contribution: Higher-level service framework on top of Finagle used to build Twitter's recommendation microservices including Home Mixer.

title: Twitter Cortex (ML platform)
abstract: Twitter Cortex was Twitter's internal machine learning platform that provided ML infrastructure and tools for the company's data science and engineering teams. The platform supported various ML workloads including model training, serving, and experimentation across Twitter's product lines. As of the time of access, the site returns a 404 error, indicating the platform's web presence has been taken down following Twitter's transition to X and associated infrastructure changes. Cortex was historically responsible for powering ML capabilities across Twitter's recommendation systems, ads targeting, content moderation, and other ML-driven features.
unique_contribution: Official landing page for Twitter's ML platform, though now inaccessible

title: Twitter Cortex Publications
abstract: This page served as an index of research publications produced by Twitter's Cortex ML team, cataloging papers on topics including recommendation systems, graph neural networks, natural language processing, content moderation, and other ML research areas. The publications page would have listed academic papers authored by Twitter researchers and published at major ML/AI conferences. Like the main Cortex site, this page now returns a 404 error following the transition to X, making the full publication list inaccessible. The page historically served as a centralized directory of Twitter's ML research output.
unique_contribution: Directory of Twitter's ML research publications, now inaccessible

title: Apache Heron (stream processing)
abstract: Apache Heron is a real-time stream processing engine originally developed at Twitter as a successor to Apache Storm, featuring significant architectural improvements. The project is implemented primarily in Java (53.5%), C++ (16.5%), Python (11.3%), and JavaScript (11.2%), using Bazel as its build system. Heron was designed to handle Twitter's massive real-time event processing needs, including the 400 billion daily events that feed into the recommendation system's feature engineering pipeline. The repository has approximately 3,300 commits and requires Java 11+, Python 3.6+, and Bazel 6.0.0. Importantly, the repository was archived on March 3, 2023, and contains a RETIRED.txt file indicating the project has been retired from active development. Heron was used within Twitter for streaming data processing that powered real-time features in the recommendation pipeline.
unique_contribution: Twitter-developed stream processing engine used for real-time event processing in the recommendation pipeline

title: What Can We Learn from 'The Algorithm'
abstract: Sol Messing, a former Twitter data scientist now at NYU, provides an insider's critical analysis of Twitter's March 2023 algorithm release. He argues the transparency claims are largely performative, since without model parameters, training data, and feature sets, the code alone provides limited insight. Key technical findings include: replies carry approximately 27x the weight of a favorite, author replies count as 75 retweets, and Twitter Blue subscribers receive 2x ranking boosts among non-followers and 4x among followers. The post reveals that Twitter created dedicated instrumentation tracking Elon Musk's personal experience and monitored partisan equality across Democrat and Republican account lists. Messing raises safety concerns about exposed Trust and Safety parameters that could help bad actors circumvent detection systems. He notes that simultaneously making API access cost $500k/year while claiming transparency undermines the ability for external researchers to actually audit the system.
unique_contribution: Insider perspective from former Twitter data scientist with specific engagement weight revelations and concerns about exposed safety parameters

title: Twitter's For You Recommendation Algorithm
abstract: Sumit Kumar, a Senior ML Engineer at Meta, provides one of the most technically thorough external analyses of Twitter's recommendation algorithm. The post covers the complete ML pipeline: data sourcing (500M daily tweets, 400B real-time events using Scalding, Heron, and TSAR), candidate generation selecting 1,500 tweets via in-network (RealGraph with logistic regression) and out-of-network sources (GraphJet using SALSA random walks for ~15% of feed, SimClusters with 145,000 communities, and TwHIN knowledge graph embeddings). The ranking section details both the light ranker (logistic regression on static/real-time features, trained years ago) and the heavy ranker (MaskNet with ~48M parameters using instance-guided masks). The combined scoring formula weights ten engagement probabilities, with negative reactions penalized at -74 and reports at -369. Filtering includes spam removal, diversity penalties for same-author repetition, and Twitter Blue 4x/2x boosts. The author notes several core models were trained years ago and remain under redesign.
unique_contribution: Most comprehensive external technical breakdown covering all pipeline stages with specific parameters, formulas, and architecture details

title: Insights into Twitter Recommendation System
abstract: Tram Anh Nguyen, a former Twitter Machine Learning Engineer (2021-2023), provides insider perspective on Twitter's recommendation system. The pipeline operates through candidate generation, light ranker (logistic regression filtering ~400 candidates), and heavy ranker (MaskNet). In-network sources use RealGraph with XGBoost for engagement prediction, while out-of-network uses GraphJet, SimClusters, and TwHIN. Critical insider insights include: most candidates derive from batch jobs running hourly, limiting content freshness; tweets require approximately 8 favorites minimum for out-of-network visibility (long-tail problem); the system heavily favors followers with lots of explicit engagement signals; and the algorithm treats users as either consumers or producers, disadvantaging regular creators. A surprising finding is that the Heavy Ranker contains no content-based embeddings, relying instead on aggregate engagement metrics. Blue-verified accounts receive 2-4x multipliers that significantly override scoring weights.
unique_contribution: Insider knowledge revealing system limitations: hourly batch jobs, 8-favorite minimum for out-of-network, no content-based embeddings in heavy ranker

title: How Twitter's Algorithm Works in 2023
abstract: Steven Tey provides a practitioner-oriented analysis of Twitter's open-sourced algorithm with specific actionable findings. Key discoveries include: Tweepcred (PageRank-derived reputation scoring 0-100) penalizes low follower-to-following ratios; Twitter Blue subscribers get 4x in-network and 2x out-of-network boosts; engagement weights show replies at 54x more than likes, profile visits with engagement at 24x, and mutual reply engagement at 150x; reports devastate visibility at -738x a like's value. Users with Tweepcred scores above 65 bypass the 3-tweet consideration limit. Tweets have a 360-minute half-life with 50% relevance decay every 6 hours. The post also covers government intervention capabilities (GovernmentRequested class), media boosts (2x for images/videos), and upcoming bookmark functionality expansion. The analysis is heavily code-referenced with specific constants and multipliers extracted from the source.
unique_contribution: Specific scoring constants extracted from code: 150x mutual reply weight, 360-min half-life, Tweepcred 65 threshold, GovernmentRequested class

title: Twitter Algorithm Source Code Explained
abstract: TweetHunter's analysis of Twitter's algorithm source code focuses on engagement scoring and practical implications. The post reports engagement multipliers: likes receive 30x boost, retweets 20x, and replies 1x. Media content (images/videos) provides a 2x multiplier. Misspellings are flagged as unknown language with minimal ranking value, meaning proper spelling significantly impacts reach. Negative signals include blocks, mutes, spam reports, and abuse reports, with unfollows carrying lesser penalties. The system uses content-based user clustering (news, entertainment, sports) and posting out-of-cluster content reduces algorithmic distribution. The system relies on three primary data inputs: engagement metrics, user behavior signals, and follower relationships. The post notes that bookmarks' impact and whether links reduce ranking remain undisclosed in the released code.
unique_contribution: Highlights user clustering by content type and impact of language/spelling on ranking

title: Cracking the Code: How the Twitter Algorithm Works
abstract: TweetHunter's comprehensive analysis covers Twitter's three-stage recommendation pipeline. Candidate sourcing splits 50% in-network (via RealGraph) and 50% out-of-network (via SimClusters with community discovery). The ranking system details engagement multipliers: likes 30x, retweets 20x, replies 1x, images/videos 2x, Twitter Blue 2x, with separate delivery-time weights showing replies at 27x vs likes at 0.5x. Tweepcred scoring below 65 limits tweet consideration to 3 tweets maximum, incorporating follower-to-following ratio, verification status, and ban history. Negative penalties include reports at -369x and blocks/mutes at -74x. Tweets have a 360-minute half-life. Practical recommendations include maintaining follower ratios (following <= 60% of followers), engaging within first 6 hours, including visual content, building niche authority, responding to replies immediately, and avoiding external links unless engagement is guaranteed.
unique_contribution: Distinguishes between training-time engagement multipliers (likes 30x) and delivery-time weights (replies 27x), practical growth recommendations

title: Everything We Learned About the Twitter Algorithm So Far
abstract: Ahmad Moussa provides a broad analysis of Twitter's open-sourced recommendation algorithm, covering the three-stage pipeline: candidate sourcing (RealGraph for in-network, GraphJet with SALSA for out-of-network, SimClusters with 145,000 communities providing ~85% of out-of-network content), ranking (Earlybird light ranker with logistic regression and MaskNet heavy ranker), and heuristics/filtering. Engagement factors extracted from the code include likes at 30x, retweets at 20x, images/videos at 2x, English UI preference at 0.5x, and URL presence negatively impacting ranking. The author offers critical commentary: the released code is a 'highly redacted version' missing crucial bias and manipulation details, URL penalization and paid subscriber prioritization contradict Musk's free-speech philosophy, API pricing changes ($100+/month) restrict independent research, and the algorithm appears 'incredibly bloated and convoluted.' Questions remain about whether released code matches production systems.
unique_contribution: Critical perspective on code completeness and production fidelity, notes SimClusters provide 85% of out-of-network content

title: Going Viral on Twitter by Reverse-Engineering The Algorithm
abstract: This post reverse-engineers Twitter's recommendation algorithm focusing on the four-stage pipeline: candidate generation (~1,500 tweets), feature extraction (~6,000 features), ML ranking via the Heavy Ranker, and final filtering with diversity rules. Key reverse-engineering insights include: engagement uses logarithmic scaling with log2(1 + engagement_count), making early engagements exponentially more important than later ones (first retweet provides 100% value, eighth adds only 17%). The TwEEPCred reputation system penalizes accounts with following-to-follower ratios exceeding 0.6 through exponential dampening: score / exp(5 * (ratio - 0.6)), with verified accounts receiving fixed scores of 100. Critical penalties include reports at -20,000 and 'Not Interested' at -1,000. Practical implications emphasize early engagement velocity, author replies receiving special weighting, 50%+ video completion optimization, and follower ratio management.
unique_contribution: Reveals logarithmic engagement scaling formula and exact TwEEPCred dampening function with ratio threshold of 0.6

title: Unveiling Tweepcred: The Power Behind Twitter's Recommendation Engine
abstract: This article provides a focused deep dive into Tweepcred, Twitter's social network analysis tool that measures user influence through a PageRank-adapted reputation scoring system. Users function as graph nodes with interactions (mentions, retweets) as edges, and the algorithm iteratively distributes influence scores until convergence. The implementation involves several Scala classes: UserMass computes initial user mass from account age, follower count, following count, device usage, and verification status; PreparePageRankData readies graph data with starting PageRank values; WeightedPageRank executes iterative computation testing for convergence; ExtractTweepcred adjusts scores based on follower-to-following ratios; and Reputation scales raw PageRank into 0-100 scores. Primary ranking factors include mentions/retweets from influential users, quality network connections, consistent platform activity, healthy follower ratios, and verified account status.
unique_contribution: Detailed breakdown of Tweepcred Scala implementation classes (UserMass, PreparePageRankData, WeightedPageRank, ExtractTweepcred, Reputation)

title: Twitter showed us its algorithm. What does it tell us?
abstract: Arvind Narayanan, Princeton CS professor, provides a scholarly analysis of Twitter's algorithm open-source release, examining what code transparency actually reveals about content amplification. He argues that releasing training code has inherent limitations since trained ML models cannot be shared due to privacy concerns, and training code alone is 'of limited value for those who want to understand information propagation on social media.' The crucial behavioral patterns in training data determine recommendations but remain inaccessible. He notes Twitter omitted training code for most content moderation classifiers, creating a major transparency gap especially as borderline violations are increasingly downranked rather than removed. Technical findings include the engagement weighting formula, Twitter's avoidance of optimizing for dwell time (which could amplify sensationalism), and the substantial weight given to negative feedback signals. He advocates for releasing configuration details (signals, engagement definitions, parameters) separately from code, arguing meaningful algorithmic transparency requires three components: configuration details, trained models, and research on user-algorithm feedback loops.
unique_contribution: Framework for meaningful algorithmic transparency: configuration details + trained models + feedback loop research; notes Twitter avoids dwell-time optimization

title: X's Open Source Algorithm -- Unveiling the Code, But Not the Secrets
abstract: Shaped AI analyzes Twitter's open-source algorithm release, focusing on the gap between what was released and what remains hidden. The post details the four-stage recommendation system: candidate sourcing (in-network and out-of-network via social graphs and embeddings), ranking (neural network with ~48M parameters trained on engagement metrics), filtering (visibility controls, author diversity, content balance), and mixing/serving (Home Mixer combining tweets with ads). The primary critique is that while code structure was released, Twitter withheld the model weights that determine specific content appearances. The release focused only on the model training pipeline, excluding specific weights or criteria for content promotion. This means developers can understand how the system works structurally but not why particular content gets promoted, leaving the most consequential details proprietary.
unique_contribution: Clear articulation of the code-vs-weights distinction and why structural understanding without weights has limited practical value

title: Twitter's Transparency Theater
abstract: NYU's Center for Social Media and Politics (CSMAP) critiques Elon Musk's transparency claims as performative. Researcher Sol Messing argues the open-sourced code lacks crucial components: 'You can't learn much from this release in and of itself' without underlying model features and parameters. The center highlights a key contradiction: while releasing algorithm code, Twitter simultaneously made auditing incredibly difficult by restricting researcher data access. API pricing shifted from free access to charges starting at $42,000 monthly for 50 million tweets, down from researchers previously collecting over 3 million tweets daily at no cost. This threatens hundreds of public interest research projects studying misinformation, health issues, and political behavior. The combination of incomplete algorithm disclosure and expensive API restrictions demonstrates what the article terms 'transparency theater' -- gestures toward openness lacking meaningful substance for independent research and public accountability.
unique_contribution: Quantifies the research impact: API pricing from free to $42k/month, threatening hundreds of public interest research projects

title: What Can We Learn From Twitter's Open Source Algorithm?
abstract: Sol Messing at NYU CSMAP argues Twitter's algorithm release is more theatrical than substantive. The released code requires underlying model details and data to be useful, while Twitter simultaneously made data access prohibitively expensive. Key concrete findings from the code include: the ranking formula showing replies carry 27x the weight of retweets and author-replied interactions counting as 75 retweets; Twitter Blue subscribers receiving 2-4x ranking boosts; special metrics tracking Elon Musk's personal experience; and Democrat/Republican account lists monitored for 'partisan equality.' Safety concerns include exposed Trust and Safety code revealing detection methods like 'HighCryptospamScore' that bad actors could exploit. Messing contends the release functions as a distraction from API restrictions rather than genuine algorithmic transparency, preventing external researchers from actually auditing the system's fairness and impact.
unique_contribution: Reveals specific Musk-tracking metrics, partisan monitoring lists, and exposed safety detection methods (HighCryptospamScore)

title: Deep Dive: Inside X's Recommendation Algorithm
abstract: Gowtham Boyina provides a detailed technical breakdown of X's recommendation algorithm covering the three-stage architecture. Stage 1 candidate sourcing reduces ~500M tweets to ~1,500 via four parallel pipelines: Search Index (RealGraph, Trust & Safety, TweepCred), CR Mixer (SimClusters, TwHIN with 1.5B nodes, GraphJet), UTEG (topic/entity engagement history), and FRS (follow recommendations to break filter bubbles). Stage 2 heavy ranker uses a neural network scoring engagement likelihood across likes, retweets, replies, clicks, and negative signals. Stage 3 applies rule-based heuristics including social proof requirements, author diversity limits, Trust & Safety checks, content balance, and feedback fatigue prevention. Feature engineering leverages GraphJet features, SimClusters embeddings, TwHIN representations (128-dimensional vectors), and RealGraph predictions. Infrastructure combines real-time processing (Manhattan, Kafka) with batch systems (Hadoop, TensorFlow). The author notes inherent biases: recency preference, popularity amplification, echo chambers, and engagement optimization over information quality.
unique_contribution: Identifies four parallel candidate sourcing pipelines (Search Index, CR Mixer, UTEG, FRS) and TwHIN's 1.5B-node scale with 128-dim vectors

title: How Twitter's Algorithm Works: A High-level Breakdown
abstract: Arnav Gupta provides a high-level overview of Twitter's recommendation system based on the March 2023 source code release. The three-stage pipeline covers: sourcing relevant tweets (in-network via RealGraph measuring relationship strength, out-of-network via GraphJet processing up to one million graph edges per second, and SimClusters embedding spaces); ranking via the Heavy Ranker neural network evaluating tweet features (text, engagement, timestamp, language) and user features (follower count, verification status) with specific weights like 11 for 2+ minutes in conversation and -369 for reporting; and filtering through author diversity, visibility filtering, content balance, and social proof heuristics. The post is accessible but lacks depth on implementation specifics, focusing more on conceptual understanding of each stage.
unique_contribution: Notes GraphJet processes up to 1 million graph edges per second; mentions 2-minute conversation dwell time weight of 11

title: Twitter Algorithm is Now Public. Here's What I Learned.
abstract: Sarvesh P. examines Twitter's publicly released recommendation code (reportedly 400,000+ lines) and identifies the three core stages: candidate sourcing (5 billion daily requests, 1,500 tweets selected from hundreds of millions, 50/50 split between in-network and out-of-network using RealGraph and SimClusters), ranking (neural network with ~48M parameters scoring engagement), and filtering (author diversity, content balance, visibility controls, social proof). The post quantifies visibility boosts: replies 1x, media content 2x, Twitter Blue 2-4x, trusted user circles 3x, retweets 20x, likes 30x. Tweets lacking accompanying text or containing only URLs face reduced distribution. The analysis is surface-level but provides a quick reference for the engagement multiplier hierarchy.
unique_contribution: Quick-reference engagement multiplier hierarchy and note about text-less/URL-only tweet penalties

title: The Ultimate Guide to Getting Recommended by Twitter's Algorithm
abstract: This Dev.to guide takes a practitioner-focused approach to Twitter's algorithm, covering the three-stage pipeline (candidate retrieval, neural network ranking, quality filtering) with emphasis on actionable optimization strategies. Key scoring factors: replies are 9x more valuable than likes in training data, retweets and likes each carry 1.0x weight, video watchtime (50%+ completion) scores 0.01-0.6x, and bookmarks/shares indicate strong intent. Negative signals include reports at -20,000 max penalty, plus blocks, mutes, and 'not interested' clicks. The guide recommends optimizing for conversation (questions and replies over likes), leveraging the critical first 30 minutes and 48-hour tweet lifespan, dominating SimClusters niche communities before expanding, creating native video with hooks in the first 3 seconds, and building authentic early engagement. Includes a 4-week action plan for profile optimization, daily engagement habits, and content testing, with content types ranked by reach tier.
unique_contribution: Actionable 4-week implementation plan; notes first 30 minutes as critical window and 48-hour tweet lifespan; video completion rate thresholds

title: Earlybird: Real-Time Search at Twitter -- A Summary
abstract: AB Kedia summarizes the Earlybird paper, Twitter's real-time search and retrieval engine that powers tweet indexing and querying. Unlike traditional web search, Earlybird handles continuously arriving data with minimal latency while maintaining immediate searchability.
