The enriched representations are then aggregated across a user's tweet history to build semantically grounded user profiles. These profiles capture not just keywords but conceptual interests and entity-level preferences. The evaluation shows that semantically enriched profiles outperform keyword-based profiles for tasks like user similarity computation and interest prediction. The work is an early example of applying linked data and semantic web techniques to social media user modeling.
unique_contribution: Semantic enrichment pipeline linking tweet content to structured knowledge bases (DBpedia/Wikipedia) for richer user profile construction.

title: Twitter Message Recommendation Based on User Interest Profiles
abstract: This paper presents a system for recommending Twitter messages (tweets) to users based on constructed interest profiles. The approach builds user interest profiles by analyzing the content of tweets a user has previously engaged with, including their own tweets, retweets, and liked content. These profiles are used to score and rank incoming tweets for relevance. The recommendation framework considers both topical relevance and temporal recency, recognizing that user interests evolve over time. The system evaluates different profile construction methods and similarity metrics for matching user profiles against candidate tweets. The work addresses the information overload problem on Twitter where users follow many accounts and cannot read all incoming tweets, proposing algorithmic curation based on inferred interests.
unique_contribution: Tweet-level recommendation system using evolving user interest profiles with temporal dynamics.

title: Empirical Study of Topic Modeling in Twitter (Stanford)
abstract: This Stanford paper conducts an empirical study of applying standard topic modeling techniques, particularly Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), to Twitter data. The research investigates whether topic models designed for longer documents (articles, papers) work effectively on the extremely short and noisy text found in tweets. The study compares different strategies for aggregating tweets before applying LDA, including treating each tweet as a document, aggregating all tweets by a single user into one document, and aggregating tweets by hashtag or conversation thread. The findings reveal that naive application of LDA to individual tweets produces poor topic coherence due to the sparsity of short texts, but user-level and hashtag-level aggregation significantly improve topic quality. This is one of the earliest systematic studies of topic modeling on Twitter and influenced subsequent work on short-text topic models.
unique_contribution: Early systematic study showing that tweet aggregation strategies (user-level, hashtag-level) are critical for effective LDA topic modeling on Twitter's short texts.

title: Using Topic Models for Twitter Hashtag Recommendation (WWW 2013)
abstract: Published at WWW 2013, this paper proposes using topic models to recommend hashtags for tweets. The system trains topic models (LDA variants) on large corpora of hashtagged tweets to learn associations between textual content and hashtags. When a user composes a new tweet, the system infers the topic distribution of the tweet and recommends hashtags that are most strongly associated with those topics. The approach handles the vocabulary mismatch problem where a tweet's text may not directly contain words from relevant hashtags. The paper evaluates on a large-scale Twitter dataset and compares against baseline methods including TF-IDF similarity and translation models. Topic model-based recommendation achieves superior performance by capturing latent semantic relationships between tweet content and hashtag usage patterns.
unique_contribution: Topic model approach to hashtag recommendation that bridges vocabulary mismatch between tweet text and relevant hashtags.

title: What You Like: Generating Explainable Topical Recommendations for Twitter
abstract: This paper develops a recommendation system for Twitter that addresses content discovery among the 500+ million tweets posted daily. The system leverages social annotations (hashtags, mentions, URLs shared by users) rather than explicit ratings, which are sparse on high-volume platforms where 44% of users never tweet. The topical approach enables the system to provide human-readable explanations for its recommendations, improving user trust and acceptance of suggested content from unfamiliar sources. The methodology constructs topic-level user profiles from social annotation patterns and matches these against candidate content. The paper compares its approach against matrix factorization-based collaborative filtering, content-based, and social recommendation baselines. The explainability component is a key differentiator, as the system can articulate why a particular piece of content was recommended in terms of the user's identified topical interests.
unique_contribution: Explainable topical recommendation framework for Twitter using social annotations instead of ratings, with human-readable justifications.

title: Mutual Interest-Based Twitter Followee Recommendation Using LDA
abstract: This recent paper proposes a followee (who-to-follow) recommendation system for Twitter based on mutual interest modeling using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA). The approach identifies topical interests of both the target user and candidate followees by applying LDA topic modeling to their tweet histories. Recommendations are generated by finding candidates whose topic distributions are most similar to the target user's interests, with the 'mutual' aspect ensuring that both parties share overlapping topical interests rather than one-directional similarity. The paper evaluates different similarity metrics for comparing topic distributions and demonstrates improvements over baseline follow recommendation methods. This work contributes to the who-to-follow recommendation problem, which is one of the key components of Twitter's user engagement strategy.
unique_contribution: Mutual interest matching for followee recommendation using bidirectional LDA topic distribution similarity.

title: Using Tweets Embeddings for Hashtag Recommendation
abstract: This 2018 paper by Ben-Lhachemi and Nfaoui from Sidi Mohammed Ben Abdellah University proposes a hashtag recommendation system for Twitter that leverages word embeddings and density-based clustering. The approach uses pre-trained word embeddings from the Google News corpus to represent tweets as dense vectors through weighted averaging of constituent word embeddings. DBSCAN (density-based spatial clustering of applications with noise) groups syntactically and semantically similar tweets into clusters. For a new tweet, the system computes similarity between its embedding vector and cluster centroids, then recommends the top-K hashtags from the most similar clusters. The method addresses the challenge that Twitter's open hashtag creation policy makes it difficult for users to identify the most appropriate tags for their posts. The approach combines the semantic richness of word embeddings with the structure provided by unsupervised clustering to bridge the gap between tweet content and hashtag vocabulary.
unique_contribution: Combines pre-trained word embeddings with DBSCAN clustering for hashtag recommendation, avoiding the need for labeled training data.

title: Tweeki: Linking Named Entities on Twitter to a Knowledge Graph (WNUT 2020)
abstract: Published at the Sixth Workshop on Noisy User-generated Text (W-NUT 2020), this paper by Harandizadeh and Singh introduces Tweeki, an unsupervised and modular entity linking system designed specifically for Twitter. The system addresses the challenge of connecting entities mentioned in tweets to knowledge bases like WikiData, where existing approaches struggle with the short, noisy nature of social media text. Tweeki contributes three main deliverables: (1) the Tweeki entity linking system itself, which uses an unsupervised modular pipeline that avoids the maintenance burden of supervised approaches; (2) TweekiData, a large corpus of automatically-annotated tweets mapped to WikiData entities; and (3) TweekiGold, a gold-standard evaluation dataset. The system achieves performance comparable to recent state-of-the-art supervised entity linkers while being more maintainable. The authors also demonstrate a practical application: using Tweeki-annotated data to improve geolocation prediction in social media analysis tasks.
unique_contribution: Unsupervised modular entity linking system for Twitter with released gold-standard evaluation dataset and large auto-annotated corpus.

title: Using Knowledge Graphs to Explain Entity Co-occurrence in Twitter
abstract: This paper investigates why certain entities frequently co-occur in Twitter conversations and uses knowledge graphs to provide explanations for these co-occurrence patterns. The approach mines entity pairs that are mentioned together in tweets more frequently than expected by chance, then searches knowledge graph paths between these entities to find explanatory connections. For example, if two politicians are frequently mentioned together, the knowledge graph might reveal they serve on the same committee or represent the same state. The method combines statistical co-occurrence analysis on Twitter data with structured relationship traversal in knowledge graphs like DBpedia or Wikidata. The work contributes to making social media trends more interpretable and could be applied to understanding why certain topics trend together or why recommendation systems surface particular content combinations.
unique_contribution: Uses knowledge graph path traversal to explain why entities co-occur in Twitter conversations beyond statistical correlation.

title: Twitter Trends: A Ranking Algorithm Analysis on Real Time Data
abstract: Published in Expert Systems with Applications (2021) by Khan, Nasir, Nasim, Shabbir, and Mahmood, this paper analyzes trend detection and ranking algorithms applied to real-time Twitter data streams. The study explores three primary analytical techniques: TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency), CCA (Combined Component Approach), and BTM (Biterm Topic Model) for discovering and ranking trending topics and hashtags. The research develops a methodology to identify trending topics from live Twitter streams and rank them by relevance and momentum. The work demonstrates that trend detection from streaming tweets is feasible through algorithmic ranking and provides practical applications for stakeholders including investors and advertisers who need to monitor social media dynamics in real time. The paper offers a comparative analysis of different algorithmic approaches to the trends problem.
unique_contribution: Comparative analysis of TF-IDF, CCA, and Biterm Topic Model approaches for real-time Twitter trend detection and ranking.

title: Twitter-Based Recommender System to Address Cold-Start: Genetic Algorithm Based Trust Modelling
abstract: This IEEE paper proposes a recommender system that uses Twitter social connections to address the cold-start problem in recommendation systems. When a new user joins a platform and has no interaction history, traditional collaborative filtering fails. The approach leverages the user's existing Twitter network to bootstrap trust models using genetic algorithms. Trust between users is computed based on their Twitter relationship signals (following, mutual following, retweet frequency, mention patterns). A genetic algorithm optimizes the weights of different trust signals to produce the most effective trust-based recommendations. The system demonstrates that social media connections provide valuable initialization signals for recommendation systems, enabling reasonable recommendations even for users with zero in-platform interaction history. The work bridges social trust modeling with evolutionary optimization for the cold-start recommendation problem.
unique_contribution: Genetic algorithm optimization of Twitter-derived trust signals to solve cold-start recommendation problem.

title: Addressing Cold-Start in App Recommendation: Latent User Models from Twitter Followers
abstract: This paper from the National University of Singapore addresses the cold-start problem in mobile app recommendation by constructing latent user models from Twitter follower networks. When a user has no app usage history, the system examines which Twitter accounts they follow to infer interests and preferences. The approach builds latent factor models from the follow graph, where each Twitter account a user follows contributes signals about their interests. These latent user representations are then mapped to app categories and specific apps that match the inferred interest profile. The methodology demonstrates that the Twitter social graph is a rich source of auxiliary information for bootstrapping recommendations in domains outside Twitter itself. The paper evaluates on real app download data and shows significant improvements over non-personalized baselines for cold-start users.
unique_contribution: Uses Twitter follower graph as auxiliary signal to build latent user models for cold-start app recommendation in a different domain.

title: Explainable ML Pipeline for Twitter Bot Detection During 2020 US Presidential Elections
abstract: Published in 2022 by Shevtsov, Tzagkarakis, Antonakaki, and Ioannidis, this paper presents a reproducible machine learning pipeline for detecting Twitter bots during the 2020 US Presidential Elections. The system processes hundreds of features extracted from tweets and user accounts, feeding them into multiple state-of-the-art ML models. After comparative evaluation, eXtreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) was selected as the best-performing classifier, demonstrating superiority in terms of high bot detection accuracy across distinct Twitter datasets. The pipeline emphasizes explainability, allowing analysts to understand which features contribute most to bot classification decisions. The work also emphasizes reproducibility, with the system certified through Code Ocean. The research contributes a practical, validated framework for bot identification during politically significant periods and provides insights into the characteristics that distinguish automated bot accounts from genuine human users.
unique_contribution: Reproducible, explainable bot detection pipeline using XGBoost with hundreds of features, validated during US elections with Code Ocean certification.

title: Spam Bot Detection on Twitter Using Positional Attention Dense CNN
abstract: This 2025 paper by Shah and Joshi from Gujarat University proposes a deep learning framework for detecting spam bots on Twitter using a Positional Attention-based Dense CNN (PAtt_Dense CNN). The approach comprises four stages: (1) pre-processing including stop word removal, tokenization, stemming, n-gram analysis, and removal of mentions/URLs/hashtags; (2) feature extraction using N-gram, TF-IDF, character-level vectorizers, and Extended BERT (EBERT); (3) feature selection via Binary Water Wheel Plant Optimization for dimensionality reduction; and (4) classification using the proposed PAtt_Dense CNN architecture. The system achieved strong performance metrics: 97.8% accuracy, 97.2% precision, 98.3% recall, and 99.2% F1-score. The work addresses the problem of bots transmitting fake news, fake ideas, and fake products on Twitter, proposing a novel neural architecture that combines positional attention mechanisms with dense convolutional layers.
unique_contribution: Novel PAtt_Dense CNN architecture combining positional attention with dense CNN for bot detection, plus Binary Water Wheel Plant Optimization for feature selection.

title: A Framework for Real-Time Spam Detection in Twitter
abstract: This paper presents a framework for detecting spam in Twitter in real time. The system processes incoming tweets and user account metadata through a feature extraction pipeline that captures both content-based features (URL patterns, hashtag density, text characteristics) and account-based features (account age, follower/following ratio, posting frequency). These features are fed into machine learning classifiers that make real-time spam/not-spam decisions. The framework is designed for low-latency operation to enable blocking or flagging spam content before it reaches users' timelines. The paper evaluates different classifier architectures for the real-time constraint and analyzes the trade-off between detection accuracy and processing speed. The work addresses a core trust and safety challenge for Twitter's recommendation system, as spam content that passes through the filter would degrade the quality of candidate generation and ranking stages.
unique_contribution: Real-time spam detection framework balancing classification accuracy with processing latency requirements for Twitter-scale operation.

title: Regulating Disinformation on Twitter and Facebook
abstract: This paper examines the regulatory landscape around disinformation on Twitter and Facebook, analyzing existing and proposed legal frameworks for governing misinformation on social media platforms. The work discusses how platform algorithms contribute to the spread of disinformation through engagement-optimizing recommendation systems that may amplify sensational or misleading content. The paper reviews regulatory approaches from multiple jurisdictions, including the EU's Digital Services Act, and evaluates their effectiveness in addressing algorithmic amplification of disinformation. The analysis covers both platform self-regulation efforts (content moderation policies, fact-checking partnerships) and governmental regulatory interventions. Rather than proposing technical solutions, the paper provides a legal and policy analysis of the regulatory challenges posed by algorithmic content curation on major social media platforms.
unique_contribution: Legal and regulatory analysis of disinformation governance frameworks as applied to Twitter and Facebook's algorithmic content curation.

title: How Twitter (X)'s Birdwatch Algorithm Works
abstract: This blog post explains how Twitter/X's Birdwatch (now Community Notes) algorithm works to combat misinformation. Birdwatch is a crowd-sourced fact-checking system where users can write notes on potentially misleading tweets. The algorithm uses a matrix factorization approach to determine which notes are shown publicly: rather than simple majority voting, the system identifies notes that are rated as helpful by users across different political viewpoints and perspectives. The matrix factorization model learns latent factors for both note raters and notes, and a note is displayed only if it receives positive ratings from raters spanning different factor positions (bridging-based ranking). This design prevents any single ideological group from controlling which notes appear. The post likely covers the open-source nature of the Community Notes algorithm and how the scoring formula determines note visibility.
unique_contribution: Accessible explanation of Community Notes' bridging-based matrix factorization algorithm for cross-partisan consensus on note helpfulness.

title: Platform Visibility and Content Moderation: Algorithms, Shadow Bans & Governance
abstract: Written by Adnan Masood, PhD (published May 2025, ~27 min read), this article examines how major social platforms including Twitter/X, Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube control content visibility through algorithmic ranking and moderation policies. The article defines shadow banning as 'stealthy visibility suppression - initially an old forum trick, now an umbrella term for hidden down-ranking and delisting.' It explores automated ML-based rankers that prioritize engagement while incorporating integrity signals to demote content flagged for hate speech, misinformation, or policy violations. The regulatory context references Europe's Digital Services Act, Germany's NetzDG, and India's IT Rules. Facebook's XCheck program for verified accounts is discussed. However, the article does not contain deep specific algorithmic details about Twitter/X's internal recommendation or moderation systems, instead treating Twitter as one of several platforms in a broader governance discussion.
unique_contribution: Cross-platform survey of visibility suppression mechanisms and regulatory frameworks, with shadow banning taxonomy.

title: Tweet Retweet Prediction Based on Deep Multitask Learning (CH-Transformer)
abstract: This paper proposes a deep multitask learning framework with a CH-Transformer architecture for predicting tweet retweet behavior. The system jointly models multiple engagement signals (retweet, like, reply) as related tasks, leveraging shared representations to improve prediction accuracy for the primary retweet prediction task. The CH-Transformer (likely a variant combining CNN, hierarchical attention, and transformer components) processes tweet content, user features, and contextual signals to produce engagement probability predictions. The multitask learning approach allows the model to learn from correlated engagement behaviors, where signals from easier-to-predict tasks (like) can improve performance on harder tasks (retweet). This work is directly relevant to Twitter's recommendation system as engagement prediction (particularly retweet probability) is one of the key signals used by the heavy ranker to score and rank candidate tweets for the home timeline.
unique_contribution: Multitask learning framework combining CH-Transformer architecture for joint modeling of multiple tweet engagement prediction tasks.
